We gather several of these readers together, the program's director Dr. Latoya Bosworth, Dr. Toni McComb, Javier Luengo-Garrido, Prof. Ousmane Power-Greene, and Anika Lopes, to share a bit of the work and talk about why it remains important. We remembered that we have never tasted wine in Franklin County, and felt we needed to fix that immediately. So we head off to Ashfield Lake House (https://www.theashfieldlakehouse.com/) , where proprietor Dre Rawlings (https://www.instagram.com/theashfieldlakehouse/) comes to our rescue for the Tina Turner Memorial Wine Thunderdome. It is our second blind tasting, but in a neat twist, we only use wines from the restaurant's cellar. And if you've enjoyed our opening theme music for the show, today's your lucky day because the band that made that song is our guest for Live Music Friday. Spouse (https://spouse.bandcamp.com/) plays Gateway City Arts (https://www.gatewaycityarts.com/event/spouse-with-bunnies-redvane/) on June 30th (right after the show in fact) (https://www.gatewaycityarts.com/event/spouse-with-bunnies-redvane/) but made a quick side stop to come in and play our theme music in studio. Spouse isn't the largest group we've ever had in studio, but they are the loudest and have the most gear, and we wouldn't have it any other way.

episodes.title: Antenna chickens in drag
episodes.text: The people we bring together are closer than you think. It happens somewhat ironically, in the portion of Rep. Jim McGovern's (https://mcgovern.house.gov) district that isn't actually in the 413, where he's just completed a round of town halls. In this week's mcgoverning with McGovern we find out about his constituents on the other side, and more solidarity with the LGBTQIA+ and drag communities. If you've got a question for the congressman, email us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or text to [800-639-9120](tel:8006399120) and we'll ask it during the next show! It happens in eating local also. Karl Prahl recently moved his poultry operation, Underline Farm (https://www.underlinefarm.com/) , to Easthampton, and in doing so landed directly across the street from the home of Vincent Corsello, who owns Corsello Butcheria (https://www.corsellobutcheria.com/) . What happens next is a kismet of community coming together to be even more local, even more present in the way we handle livestock, and even more dedicated to making sure the impact of the industries we're involved in is beneficial to everyone. And it is a mainstay of making music. The Antenna Cloud Farm's Music Walk takes place July 1st (https://www.antennacloudfarm.com/2023-music-walk) in Downtown Turners/Great Falls, filling the town with innovative and interesting music from 2-8pm, both with in-person musicians and virtually in self guided tours. We hear a sample of the performers in Aisha Burns (http://aishaburns.com/) who is scheduled to play that evening, and hear more about how the Antenna Cloud Farm (https://www.antennacloudfarm.com/) brings folx together to make such a fun event happen from founder Michi Wiancko (https://www.michiwiancko.com/) (who you may have heard in the NERO Orchestra episode (https://www.nepm.org/podcast/the-fabulous-413/2023-05-09/our-family-orchestra)

episodes.title: Good kids songs for bears feeling badly
episodes.text: This show is for babies. Just kidding, but it is about young ones, and that's almost as cute. First there's the music for kids and their grown-ups happening at The Parlor Room in Northampton. Liam Hurley and Nick Deysher (https://www.inthenickoftimemusic.com/) , also known as Mr. Liam and Mr. Nick, curate The Parlor Room Kids Series (https://parlorroom.org/#calendar-38e4e131-72d9-4b2c-96d9-ed3e3c054ab2-event-ljghkubh) , which happens every first Sunday of the month on a sliding scale so that everyone can be included. This is kids music for everyone, parents included, and we get a taste of what's in store for those families this Sunday (https://www.simpletix.com/e/parlor-room-kids-series-mr-liam-nick-and-t-tickets-136789) when they stop by the studios, ukeleles in tow to evoke a couple 10 second dance parties for us. Then there's bear cubs. When young bears are orphaned, sometimes Kilham Bear Center (https://kilhambearcenter.org/) in New Hampshire steps in to care for them until they can be released. If that seems a little far for our 413 focus, it turns out that they took in two cubs from Massachusetts, including a bear discovered in Greenfield that they named Alma. NEPM senior reporter Nancy Cohen (https://www.nepm.org/people/nancy-eve-cohen) has been following the story of Alma ever since its placement in the facility, so we chat with her about the things that happen to go along with Alma's release back into the wild this spring. And is it more mature to say that you feel bad or that you feel badly? We ask The Word Nerd, resident wordster Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) , senior editor at Merriam-Webster (https://merriam-webster.com/) about which phrase is more correct, and what other phrases like that have become contentious. If you have a linguistic question for The Word Nerd, let us know! Email us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org)

episodes.title: A parade a-brewing
episodes.text: Community comes in all shapes and sizes and that's what we love best about it. It comes in the form of a long standing pillar of the coffee community turning his company over to his employees. Dean Cycon of Dean's Beans Organic Coffee (https://deansbeans.com/) announced his retirement a while ago, but his official last day was June 26th. And with that last day, the company became a worker-owned co-operative (https://deansbeans.com/blogs/javatrekker-blog-news/all-the-stars-are-aligning-weve-got-huge-news) . We speak with Dean and the company's new CEO Beth Spong about making that shift happen and the legacy of societal change they've been able to enact in the coffee industry stateside and with their international partners. And pirates. Arrr. And it comes in the form of a long loved cultural celebration returning to the streets. The Springfield Puerto Rican Parade (https://www.springfieldprparade.com/) has been a fixture for over 33 years. Back in person and out on the streets, the event will have a kickoff party June 29th (https://www.springfieldprparade.com/honorees) at White Lion (https://www.whitelionbrewing.com/) . To get a better handle on all of the fun things you can do in it's 2+ mile stretch downtown, we invited parade administrator Shirley Rodríguez (https://shirleyrodriguez.homeslogic.com) and NEPM's own Elizabeth Román (https://www.nepm.org/people/elizabeth-roman) into the studio to share their experiences and talk about the future of the parade.

episodes.title: Asteroid Festivals
episodes.text: We're still reeling from the awesome that was this weekend, and in that way, today's show is a bit of a recap. First we head to Amherst to check out the new Wes Anderson movie, "Asteroid City". We get to speak with executive director of Amherst Cinema (https://amherstcinema.org) Yasmin Chin Eisenhauer about some of the events coming to their theater, and then delve into the science-y-ness of the film with Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed. (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) We promise no spoilers for the film. Next, a set of performances we held at the Green River Festival. (https://www.greenriverfestival.com/) Taking place in the Roundhouse in the middle of the Franklin County Fairgrounds, we cajoled a handful of the artists already performing at the festival to perform impromptu intimate performances and chat with us. We sit with The Wood Brothers (https://www.thewoodbros.com) , who mostly are brothers and talk about the instrument the not-quite-brother of the band has created out of an old guitar and many other things as well as how their latest album "Heart Is the Hero" (https://www.thewoodbros.com/music) came to be recorded. And we get to hang out with Vivian Leva and Riley Calcagno who make up the folk duo Viv & Riley (https://vivandriley.com) , just before Riley sat in on Raichel Baiman's set at the Back Porch Stage. We got to explore the world of old-fashioned string band sound as they continue to update it for a new millenium.

episodes.title: LIVE from Green River Festival!
episodes.text: It's Green River Festival Weekend (https://www.greenriverfestival.com/) ! And we're broadcasting live from the Back Porch Stage! We chat with Jim Olsen, the man behind the whole thing as well as Signature Sounds (https://www.signaturesoundspresents.com/) in Northampton about the history of the festival and the acts that have gotten him excited over the course of his tenure. We get Nan Perrotti, artistic director of the festival, whose distinctive style is easily identified on signs at many events, to talk to us about how she came to work her magic in script and how she came to be in the 413 as well. Kent Hunt of East Branch Studio (https://www.eastbranchstudio.com/) joins us to talk about building the Green House stage: a tiny house that is used for the festival and then repurposed. Professor Rob Williams (https://www.umass.edu/architecture/member/robert-williams) and several students from the 5 colleges involved with the project arrived with him to explore why a construction effort of this nature is important not just to the festival itself, but to our community building as well. And it wouldn't be the Green River Festival without music. So Bridget Kearney (http://www.bridgetkearney.com/) makes her way to the stage just before her solo set, as does Suitcase Junket (https://thesuitcasejunket.com/) , fresh from his completion of his secret show for the campsite.

episodes.title: Wait wait, don't fix it.
episodes.text: Technical difficulties are our favorite, let us tell you. But that doesn't stop us from chatting with Negin Farsad (https://neginfarsad.com/bio/) and Roy Blount Jr. (https://royblountjr.com/bio/) of NPR's "Wait Wait Don't Tell Me" (https://www.npr.org/2010/12/10/110997427/about-wait-wait-don-t-tell-me) just before they headed to the Berkshires for a live taping of the show. We subject them to a barrage of quiz adjacent questions as a warm up to the show on which they are regular guests, and broach the subject of farm animal sounds in various countries. Another part of our lemonade making involves Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov) , who joins us for our weekly mcgoverning. We get his take on the Schiff censure (https://www.npr.org/2023/06/21/1183564794/schiff-censure-house-republicans) , the rolling attempts at impeachment, and the Poor People's Campaign (https://www.poorpeoplescampaign.org/) . If you have a question for the congressman, email us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or text us at [1800-639-9120](tel:18006399120) and we’ll ask him on air. And for everyone not spending their whole weekend at the Franklin County Fairgrounds, we give a quick plywood ramp to the weekend, full of fun things to do in the 4 counties that isn't the Green River Festival. (https://www.greenriverfestival.com/)

episodes.title: Beside the secret weather
episodes.text: It's finally summer! Astrologically, that is. We chat with local armchair meteorology expert Dave Hayes The Weather Nut (https://westernmassweather.com/) about the differences between the summer of space and the summer of the skies and what we should expect at the Green River Festival this weekend. Local multi-disciplinary artist Terry Janoure (https://www.terryjenoure.com/) is embarking on a new musical journey at Bombyx on June 25th (https://bombyx.live/events/secret-to-life/) . This new work titled "Secret to Life" highlights the lives of older women of color and is based on an ongoing series of interviews Janoure conducts. We talk with her and Linda McInerney (https://www.eggtooth.org/about) of Eggtooth Productions (https://www.eggtooth.org/) , who are helping to bring this to the stage, about the importance of these stories and their retelling through music. And we're joined by The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) , Senior Editor at Merriam-Webster (https://merriam-webster.com/) who guides our attention to a word they have been watching for a while: "parasocial". And we'll get into how a word might incur the watchful eye of our local dictionary here in Springfield.

episodes.title: Bridge St Grows Up
episodes.text: We are back at full host capacity! Huzzah! But someone wasn't able to fully turn off while vacationing due to seeing a logo that referenced Western Mass. Monte chats with Tim Convery of Tim-Scapes (https://tim-scapes.com) about his city designs including the one for "HAMP", and other inspirations. Returning is hungry work, however, so we get a visit from Clarissa Lee-Madden of Grown Up Farm in Belchertown (https://www.grownupfarm.com/) . Though this farm started out in mycology, it's branched out much more since it's origins and we get into what's good that's coming up from the ground right now, as well as some extra delicious ways to eat them from the personal chef herself. (https://www.grownupfarm.com/chef-services) And speaking of eating, we also visit Bridge Street School (https://northamptonschools.org/project/bridge-street-school/) 's Better World Picnic. This annual celebration is not just in honor of the end of the school year, but also of the rich diversity that the student body and their families represent.

episodes.title: Compost arises
episodes.text: We are here for the uplift. It's found in the many Juneteenth celebrations happening next week, and especially in the one being thrown by Arise for Social Justice (https://www.arisespringfield.org/) in Springfield. Founded 30 years ago, Arise has been a part of several initiatives for bettering the city, including opposing the biomass plant. We speak with Rusty Polsgrove about the importance of celebrating as a part of resistance (https://www.instagram.com/p/CsIHeKfMQLy/) , and how collaboration helps further causes faster. Plus that uplift is very much at the core of The Compost Cooperative (https://thecompostcooperative.com/) as well. We continue our conversation with worker-owners Trenda Loftin (http://www.trendaloftin.com/) and Revan Schendler about the progress the co-op has made with their housing project, and about the inherent flaws in our systems that their business model is actively attempting to combat.

episodes.title: Cooperative geekery
episodes.text: Today was all about building the communities you need to see in the world. We take a brief breather from our Juneteenth gear up to look to the north at the things the Compost Cooperative (https://thecompostcooperative.com/) is up to. Worker-owners Revan Schendler and Trenda Loftin (https://www.willowprec.com/) swing through the studios to tell us more about the core mission of the co-operative and how their model seeks to combat recidivism and climate change at the same time. Then we brush the surface of nerdom, a topic near and dear to Kaliis' heart. The BEGIN Podcast (https://soundcloud.com/begin-podcast?fbclid=IwAR0Lr4r-spmukswwMCdpol9lTNyCXFpRrz3Hhegmae0erNHnlrXR4gSAe3w) features black nerds in western Mass digging into all things geeky. Two of the hosts, Neal Robinson and Garrick Perry (https://www.garrickperryward4.com/) , stop by to chat with Kaliis about our respective histories on what used to be the outskirts of society, and the intersectionality of black and nerd culture.

episodes.title: Bluesmen on the high Greenfield seas
episodes.text: We're checking out the things that remain. It's in the rumors of a bluesman that stay with us to this day. Ms. Annye Anderson has made a mission out of dispelling those tales about her step-brother, Robert Johnson, in her book "Brother Robert: Growing Up With Robert Johnson" (https://www.hachettebookgroup.com/titles/annye-c-anderson/brother-robert/9780306845260/?lens=hachette-books) . But her life is just as storied and interesting as his is, and we find out about both. It's in the celebrations we make as well. Juneteenth comes to Greenfield this Monday (https://visitgreenfieldma.com/event/lets-celebrate-juneteenth/) in an event thrown by Moving Mountains Media (https://movingmountainsmedia.org/) . The festivities are in their second year and we bring Mpress Bennu, the organizer behind the event, to the studios to talk about the happenings both new and famililar that you can encounter on our day of freedom. It's the parts of the language we keep. The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster, (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) resident wordster and senior editor from Merriam-Webster (https://merriam-webster.com/) shows us land-lubbers that some of our common phrases washed ashore from the sea, managing to evolve and thrive away from the briny depths and ships they originally sailed upon. If you've got a question you'd like to ask of The Word Nerd, send it to us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or text to [1-800-639-9120](tel:18006399120) anytime

episodes.title: History held in common
episodes.text: Sometimes it's good to dig into the roots of how we got to where we are. It is a compelling factor that will bring Elizabeth "Mumbet" Freeman's story to life at Historic Deerfield on Juneteenth (https://www.historic-deerfield.org/events/2023/6/19/tammy-deneases-one-woman-performance-as-elizabeth-mumbet-freeman) . Tammy Denease of Hidden Women (https://hiddenwomen.org/stage-company/) stage company presents her one woman show on Freeman's life. But it's just one of the many lives she portrays, so we speak about using theater as a tool to educate about history. It's where we are too. As Wisteriahurst Museum (https://wistariahurst.org/) prepares to throw its Juneteenth celebrations on the holiday itself, we're joined by Erika Slocumb, a historian at the museum. In addition to organizing their Juneteenth event (https://wistariahurst.org/events/celebrate-juneteenth-a-day-of-freedom/) , she's been working on a project that looks to highlight and preserve the oral history of the black community in Holyoke (https://wistariahurst.org/the-black-holyoke-project-uncovering-the-history-of-the-black-community-in-the-paper-city/) , but has found that the reaches of community stretch much farther than she'd originally imagined. And it's in physical roots, including some of the ones we eat. We sat down with Phil Korman from CISA (https://buylocalfood.org/) , Nate Shattuck of Shattuck's Sugarhouse (https://www.facebook.com/shattuckssugarhouse/) and Devon Lane Farm, and Katie Bobbin (https://www.instagram.com/cookie.joy.yoga/?hl=en) , the new manager of the Belchertown Farmers & Artisans Market (https://belchertownfarmersmarket.weebly.com/) to check in on how their opening weekend went considering that they are one of the few Sunday markets in the area, and to take a look at how this particular market has evolved over the past 30 years or so.

episodes.title: The rapt afro from space
episodes.text: Kaliis flies solo this week! No one panic!Although there was some panic about microbes from space at one point. Mr. Universe, Dr. Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) joins us live to chat about the complete futility of quarantining to prevent microorganisms that would have been brought back by space missions. But our caution about cosmic cross-contamination still influences missions to this day, and may see some results on Mars. June 12th was once declared Nelson Stevens Day in Springfield. Since the artist currently has a show up at Springfield Museums (https://springfieldmuseums.org/exhibitions/nelson-stevens-color-rapping/) , we talk with NEPM's Kari Njiri (https://www.nepm.org/people/kari-njiiri) , who not only has reported on the exhibit and the folx involved in making it happen, but has stories to tell from knowing him in everyday life. And it's the start of our week long Juneteenth ramp up. I Am Afro: A Street Fair For All People (https://berkshirebec.org/programming/) is a Juneteenth event happening this weekend in North Adams featuring a wealth of activities. The festivities are being produced through a partnership of the Berkshire Black Economic Council (https://berkshirebec.org) and the First Congregational Church of North Adams. The BBEC's president and founder, A.J. Enchill, sits with us to talk about the festivities and other civic action as well. And we're interested in finding out about your connections to Juneteenth as the week goes on. Have you celebrated it before? Is this your first time hearing about the holiday? Let us know at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or send a text to [1-800-639-9120](tel:18006399120).

episodes.title: Django's proud ceviche
episodes.text: More celebrations, more better, we say. We're celebrating a widening palate, constantly tested and rested in the Tina Turner Memorial Thunderdome, which we enter with the folx at State Street in Northampton (https://statestreetfruit.com/wine-beer-spirits/) . There we take two rosés and pit them against the seafood melange of ceviche, as the Wine Uncle and Yankee Sippah consistently up their pairing game. We're celebrating our queer community, especially in these times where we are seeing it threatened in places near and far. Heather Mahoney, Jake Krain, and Kacie Dean of Franklin County Pride (https://www.franklincountypride.org/) visit us in studio and let us know all of the wonderful events they have planed for their Pride (https://www.franklincountypride.org/events) happening all week and beyond, for all walks and ages of the LGBTQIA+ spectrum. And we're celebrating innovators. Django in June (https://djangoinjune.com/) highlights the musical style anchored by Django Reinhardt and is nearing its 20th year. For live music Friday, we're joined by organizer Andrew Lawrence (http://communityguitar.com/find/noho/aboutandrew.html) , guitarist and instructor Jack Soref (https://www.jacksoref.com/) , and clarinetist Evan Arntzen (https://www.evanarntzen.com/) who in addition to showing us the incredible virtuosity of the jazz manouche style, but key us into some of the unique perspectives this event brings to western Massachusetts.

episodes.title: The rules for saving fashionable theater
episodes.text: Isn't it lovely when community comes together to make art? We think so! It's why we speak with The Greenfield Players (https://www.facebook.com/GreenfieldPlayers/) , who for the next two weekends will bring the bard to Energy Park for their presentation of Twelfth Night (https://greenfieldrecreation.com/concertseries.html) . With company members Rachel Cronen-Townsend, Daniel Greycloud-Jacob, Steve Cronen-Townsend, Cassie Wood-Triplet, we get into the appeal of taking your Shakespeare outside for a little Vitamin D, and why this particular work continues to resonate. And it's in manner that we all wear ourselves, the hidden talents that bubble forth. Those talents are a thing that FAB Fashion Passion Week (https://www.fpfwne.com/) is actively seeking right now. This collaboration between Make It Springfield (https://makeitspringfield.org) and FAB Fashion of Turners Falls (https://richierichardsonfab.com/) is seeking to highlight local designers and hobbyists as well as global designers. Both groups are very much looking for folx in the community to participate (https://www.facebook.com/photo?fbid=705183874941885&set=a.551240637002877) ! We chat with organizers Richie Richardson of Fab Fashion (mailto:richie@richierichardsonfab.com) and Roberta Wilmore of Make It Springfield about the power and importance of globalizing our arts communities through collaborations like these on big and small scales. It's in the way we come together to preserve these spaces as well. There is currently a campaign to save the Triplex Cinema (https://www.savethetriplex.org/) in Great Barrington, which shut it's doors on June 5th. And some big names have come out in support of keeping this small but vital art-house theater operating, including Gregory Crewdson (http://www.gregorycrewdsonmovie.com/) , who through June 9th is offering prints of one of his previously unreleased works (https://aperture.org/gregorycrewdson/) as part of this fundraising effort. We chat with him about how cinematic sensibilities bleed into his work, and how he got involved in the preservation efforts. And if we're honest, making good legislature is an art as well. Which may be why Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov) has a bit of ennui today. For today's mcgoverning with McGovern, we dig into his discontent with the Rules Committee, a board for which he was chair when the democrats had a majority in the House.

episodes.title: The knees of a peaceful leviathan
episodes.text: We explore language today! What's that? We explore language every Wednesday because of The Word Nerd? To that we rebut: indeed. But how is this Wednesday different from any other Wednesday? Perhaps it's because we had a great listener question from a listener about the origins of certain English phrases (inspired by our look at the origins of the language in a previous show (https://www.nepm.org/podcast/the-fabulous-413/2023-06-01/famous-botanical-amalgams) ). So Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) , resident wordster and senior editor at Merriam-Webster (https://www.merriam-webster.com/) , dives right into the Middle-English of things to show us how some common phrases have evolved, and giving us more food for thought in the process. Or perhaps it is because we spent time with Polly Byers of the Karuna Center for Peacebuilding (https://karunacenter.org) . Their Building Resilience Against Violent Extremism in Schools (or the BRAVE Schools (https://karunacenter.org/project/building-resilience-against-violent-extremism-brave-schools/) ) program seeks to help middle and high school students, as well as the staff in those institutions, in using dialogue to deescalate instances of violence and hate. This weekend, they host "Bringing the Lessons Home:The Power of Dialogue, Locally and Globally" (https://karunacenter.org/events/) at the Amherst Women's Center to help folx see the applicability of the word to help everyone near and far. But mostly we're thinking it's because we have a bonus Live Music Wednesday. On tour from Boston, and making a stop at the Jewish Community of Amherst, is Levyosn (https://www.facebook.com/profile.php?id=100089425607212) . With their name taken from the Ashkenazi Hebrew for “Leviathan”, the group specializes in Yiddish song and klezmer, but they also sing in Hebrew, Ladino, and English and draw from adjacent Eastern European folk traditions, all of which you can hear on their brand new album "Lullabye" (https://borschtbeat.bandcamp.com/album/levyosns-lullaby) . Lucky for us, they dropped by the studio to give us a taste of that new album in person.

episodes.title: The banning of strawberry murals
episodes.text: Today is all about participation! It's exactly what we want to do in strawberry season! and since that has just started, we head to the pick-your-own fields of Pie In The Sky Farm (https://www.instagram.com/pie_in_sky_berry_farm/?hl=en) in Northampton. There, proprietors Kristen Sykes and Fred Beddall walk us through their many different varieties (some ready and some not), show us some of their other PYO crops that are just getting started, and speak to how their particular land stewardship (https://www.kestreltrust.org/berry-farm-dream-comes-true/) helps all their crops and the area itself do better. It's what we want to do with our neighborhoods too. And what better way to participate than making art! Britt Ruhe of Common Wealth Murals (https://www.commonwealthmurals.org/) swings through the studios to talk about all of the murals her organization has been bringing to Springfield. We discover how Common Wealth Murals helps to train more muralists (https://www.commonwealthmurals.org/community-mural-institute-details) , how they got involved in the Nelson Stevens restorations (https://www.gazettenet.com/Springfield-Mural-festival-to-honor-Legacy-of-Late-Artist-Educator-and-UMass-Amherst-Afro-American-Studies-Professor-Nelson-Stevens-48157698) , and how the second organization that CWA founded, Fresh Paint Springfield (https://www.freshpaintspringfield.com/) , truly gets the community involved while continuing to do bigger and better things (some this week even!) And speaking of cities, we participate in the civic process. NEPM reporter Jill Kaufman (https://www.nepm.org/people/jill-kaufman) has been reporting on a motion to censor books in Ludlow school libraries (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-05-26/ludlows-school-library-proposal-either-a-highway-to-censorship-or-a-barrier-to-pornography) . She, and author Jarret J. Krosoczka (https://www.studiojjk.com/) who has had his books banned before, sit with us to un-tease the Gordian knot of how we got here before the meeting next week that may put the sanctions into place.

episodes.title: The Asparagus Festival, Live!
episodes.text: We did a whole show live from the stage at the NEPM Asparagus Festival (https://www.nepm.org/nepm-events/2023-03-09/nepm-asparagus-festival) in Hadley! Since this is our first time trying something like this, we figure we'd pull out all the stops and have a show just jam packed with guests. So that's exactly what we do. For something as iconic to Hadley as asparagus, we need to bring along someone who's been immersed in the crop for ages. So we bring in Wally Czajkowski of Plainville Farm (https://www.plainvillevegetablefarm.com/) , who not only has been growing Hadley grass for decades, but has the most delightful way of saying it as well. We have Ashley Randle (https://www.mass.gov/service-details/about-mdar-commissioner) of the Massachusetts Department of Agricultural Resourses, Scott Soares (https://www.rd.usda.gov/about-rd/leadership/state-directors/scott-soares) of the USDA, and Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) join us to chat about the state of not just asparagus, but farming in western Massachusetts in general, especially in the fall out of a series of terrible weather events. We have a Tina Turner Memorial Wine Thunderdome with Michelle Kersbergen of Black Birch Vineyards (https://www.blackbirchvineyard.com/about/winemakers) , and a beer tasting with Jeff Goulet of Loophole Brewing (https://www.loophole.beer/) , who gets to share more about the brewery's upcoming move to Springfield. And Latin Grammy Award winning local artist Mr. G regales (https://mistergsongs.com/) us alongside the Global Citizen Ensemble. It's a wicked good time.

episodes.title: Tributes in residency
episodes.text: It's a day for acknowledgements. We're honoring the legacies of legends whose contributions can't be quantified. Such is the case with Ramsey Lewis (https://www.ramseylewis.com/) , who is being honored on June 3rd at Gateway City Arts (https://www.gatewaycityarts.com/) in Holyoke. Genuine Culture, LLC (http://genuineculturellc.com/) has gathered a group of jazz players and MCs to walk through his catalog to give a glimpse of the breadth of his influence across many genres (https://www.whosampled.com/Ramsey-Lewis/) . We speak with Damany Gordon (https://genuineculturellc.com/our-team/) about about the many innovations and connections Lewis made over the course of his life. We're honoring icons we recently lost (https://www.tinaturnerofficial.com/) by renaming the wine thunderdome for it's namesake. And under it's new heading, we head for Table and Vine (https://tableandvine.com/) in West Springfield to sip with Michael Quinlan (https://blog.tableandvine.com/contact/about/) . It's our third straight week of firsts, as we try two wines of the same type from the same vineyard and truly see the variety that terroir can bring. And we're honoring fortitude and virtuosity as well. Jeffrey Foucault (https://jeffreyfoucault.com/) starts his residency at Floodwater Brewing (http://floodwaterbrewing.com/index.html) tonight, performing for the next three Fridays, but he's also playing in the Berkshires over the weekend at the Egremont Barn. Both he and his drummer for the night, Don McAulay (https://www.moderndrummer.com/article/don-mcaulay-charlie-watts-drum-tech/) were a part of Sparkletown: a tribute to Kate Lorenz (https://sheatheater.org/d/14892/SparkleTown-A-Tribute-to-the-Music--Spirit-of-Kate-Lorenz) a few weekends back at The Shea Theater, so in addition to hearing music from his catalog, we reminisce with the two of them about loss in the musical community near and far.

episodes.title: The truth, Ruth
episodes.text: Today we're getting into the local glow-ups. There's no containing our excitement when we speak with Ruth E. Carter, (https://www.instagram.com/therealruthecarter/?hl=en) Springfield native, four-time nominee and two-time winner of an Oscar for costume design (https://www.chroniclebooks.com/products/the-art-of-ruth-e-carter) . Over fifty movies later (https://www.imdb.com/name/nm0141921/) , we're still in awe of how her vision truly brings together characters. We talk about her start in costuming, working on Marvel movies, her late mother, and the truly impressive list of accolades she's acquired over the years. And we mcgovern with Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) , who's had a nail-biting week as we narrowly escape defaulting on our payments as a nation. But the final resolution (https://www.nepm.org/2023-06-01/house-passes-debt-ceiling-bill-senate-to-review-next) didn't sit well with him and we'll find out why he voted against the measure in the end. Plus, Pride month is here! And although we'll have more on various events happening in the 4 counties of western Massachusetts soon, we give you a quick rundown of Pride events happening (https://www.springfieldprideparade.org/pride2023) this very weekend (https://www.berkshirepride.org/) in Pittsfield and Springfield! Please note: we mention in the show that Ruth E. Carter is being honored at a gala later in the month. More information about the Pink Magnolia Gala (https://www.xixiomegaaka.com/events) and the causes that supports can be found here (https://www.xixiomegaaka.com/events) .

episodes.title: Famous botanical amalgams
episodes.text: Hidden gems are always fun to find. There's cool things to discover in our very language itself. Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) , resident wordster, senior editor at Merriam-Webster (https://www.Merriam-Webster.com) in Springfield, helps us delve into the conflict ridden marriage of German and French that ultimately resulted in the modern English Language. The Battle of Hastings is very involved, but your dinner might be also. We encounter gorgeous flora at the Berkshire Botanical Gardens. (https://www.berkshirebotanical.org) Nestled in Stockbridge, spring finds the grounds literally bursting with life, and new projects to bring more plant life to western Massachusetts. Executive director Thaddeus Thompson (https://www.instagram.com/thompson_thaddeus/?hl=en) and director of horticulture Eric Ruquist (https://www.instagram.com/ericruquist/?hl=en) speak with us on the new things they've got planned, the problems climate change is posing, and reveal some of the surprises found among their 24 acres. And perhaps there's a famous face in the crowd that gets noticed. Which is what happened this weekend during Amherst College's commencement where several folx spotted Harrison Ford in the wild. So we ask you what celebrity you've encountered in the 413, and share some of our own as well. **Please note: there is humor regarding death within our talk at the Berkshire Botanical Gardens

episodes.title: Al fresco graduation in Paradise City
episodes.text: There is something to be said about just getting things done. It's not always easy, and this is an impasse that The Literacy Project (https://www.literacyproject.org/) seeks to address. In fact, on June 1st, they are holding a graduation ceremony at Greenfield Community College for folx who have just completed their GED (https://www.literacyproject.org/news/2023/5/1/graduation-2023) through programs they have in place. We chat with outgoing executive director Judith Roberts (https://www.literacyproject.org/news/2023/2/1/executive-director-judith-roberts-is-retiring) , Kat Brenton and Amos Johnson who'll be graduating this week, and their keynote speaker, Tim Lovett (https://comedyweapon.com/) , who is a graduate of the program himself, about the importance of keeping education open to everyone. It's happening in Northampton, where the 3rd Summer on Strong (https://www.summeronstrong.com) begins today, shutting down a portion of the street to instead offer outdoor dining, music, and entertainment all summer long. We interrupt them in the middle of setting up to talk about the power of eating local with Jeremy Werther of Homestead (https://eathomestead.com/) and invite Deb Flynn of Eastside Grill (https://www.eastsidegrill.com/) and Amy Cahillane of the Downtown Northampton Association (http://www.northamptondna.com) along to look at how community has made this festival happen in more ways than one. And not to double down on Northampton, but it's happening in their City Hall as well. For our third installment of "Meet the Mayor", we get to hang out with Gina-Louise Sciarra (https://www.northamptonma.gov/720/Mayors-Office) . Now that the Bombyx debacle has mostly died down, we get to ask her about the redesign of downtown, the IHEG liquor license problem (https://www.gazettenet.com/Suher-reaches-agreement-with-license-commission-51023282) , and the finer points of city budgeting.

episodes.title: Three cans of Bombyx
episodes.text: Sometimes the weekend requires a shift in perspective. Which is exactly what Provisions (https://provisionswine.com/) is aiming to do in the Wine Thunderdome. Benson Hyde and Bruce McAmis bring us a possibly contentious set of contenders as we pit two canned wine spritzers against each other, and examine one of the more sustainable routes wine may be taking forward. It's what all of us will have to take with the Bombyx Center for Arts and Equity in Florence (https://bombyx.live/) . Over the past three weeks this venue has been through logistical and bureaucratic rollercoasters. But we chat with Cassandra Holden from Bombyx and Laudable Productions (https://laudable.productions/) and Bombyx Board Chair, Elizabeth Dunaway about what exactly got them shut down, and about the steps they and the city are taking to get them opened up again. And we can even make a pivot with time signatures. In their own words, The Threesies (https://www.threesiesmusic.com) rage against the tyranny that is common or 4/4 time by taking up the underappreciated 3/4 and it's many derivatives. The band itself is a frequently rotating cast of players, but Paul Kochanski (https://www.paulkochanskibass.com/) , J.J. O'Connell, and Jim Henry (https://www.jimhenry.net/) join us (in a set of three themselves, no less) to bring more light to songs that swing.

episodes.title: An accidental oracle of synchronized children
episodes.text: Today, there is an overabundance of creativity. Scotty Swan (https://www.linkedin.com/in/scotty-swan-57584a72) is engendering it in kids. He teaches Rock Band Camp who will perform at the Northampton Center for the Arts on May 28th (https://www.nohoarts.org/events) . We chat with him about getting kids comfortable enough to really shred, and about the other camp he runs in the summer: The Urban Wizards Academy. There's a whole organization that's encouraging this creativity in older kids also. MAPS, or Music and Poetry Synchronized (https://mapsmusic.org/) has been operating for 17 years on a simple model: get two schools and have them bridge ethnic, gender, socio-economic, and environmental differences by making art together (https://www.youtube.com/mapsmusicorg) . In order to take a look at the whole program and see how it works and evolves while continuing to have more and more schools participate (https://www.tiktok.com/@mapsmusicorg) , we speak with some of the students participating this year, with the administrators and founder of the program, and the teachers getting their classes involved. And we see it in a cautionary tale of sorts. Our regular check in with Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov) is besieged by technical difficulties, and we're forced to look at the congressman's own past words to figure out a way to salvage the whole thing.

episodes.title: Iced Museums, repatriated
episodes.text: There's a little extra curation happening today. The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) , senior editor at Merriam-Webster (https://merriam-webster.com/) , and our resident wordster, alerts us that a shift is happening between our iced coffee and ice tea. Letters are disappearing from certain descriptive words in a totally natural way and we dig into why. Then we are figuring out how to un-curate, of sorts. Thousands of indigenous remains have been kept (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-05-24/dozens-of-native-remains-from-western-mass-still-in-museums-despite-decades-old-repatriation-law) in museum, school, and library collections for ages. And the rules for giving those remains back to the peoples they came from are about to change in a drastic way. We sit with NEPM's Nancy Eve Cohen (https://www.nepm.org/people/nancy-eve-cohen) to find out more about the ways NAGPRA, the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (https://www.nps.gov/subjects/nagpra/getting-started.htm) , is changing and how that affects some local tribes and institutions. And we're more than a little impressed by the curation Mass MoCA (https://massmoca.org) has done for it's summer program. From theater, to installations, to a bevvy of fantastic musicians from all over the globe, they're pulling out a lot of stops in these next few months (https://massmoca.org/calendar/) . Addison McDonald, general manager of performing arts and film, chats with us about the things he's most excited to share with us in North Adams. Plus, we reminisce on the legacy that the great Tina Turner (https://www.tinaturnerofficial.com/) leaves in her wake.

episodes.title: Dance like the trees are freezing
episodes.text: Today, we peek behind the curtain. There's synergy to be found in young folx collaborating and figuring out new paths of expression. The School of Dance and Contemporary Thought (https://www.scdtnoho.com) approaches this from several fronts: working with other youth arts group, and by fostering it's own young pre-professional company in The Hatchery (https://www.scdtnoho.com/hatchery.html) . Both prongs of this philosophy have shows (https://www.aomtheatre.com/event/the-hatchery-dance-company-in-big-time/) this weekend (https://www.aomtheatre.com/event/exchange-the-teen-performance-festival/) at the Academy of Music in Northampton and we chat with Artistic Director and Founder Jen Polins about fostering a blossoming dance culture in the valley. There's adaptations to be found in Sunderland. Dave Wisseman of Millstone Market (https://www.themillstonemarket.com/) , Warner's Farm (https://www.warnerfarm.com/) , and Mike's Maze (https://mikesmaze.com/) gives us a real look at how devastating last week's frost has been, and at how learning on the go helped them not only survive that late season freeze but the pandemic as well. Also about his love for strawberries (which mostly managed to survive). And there's growth to be found in the mountains. We head to Tanglewood (https://www.bso.org/tanglewood) to visit it's namesake. Which isn't to say that the woods are tangled, but that their tales may be. So we seek out groundskeeper Thomas Leahy (who we have affectionately dubbed the Lorax-in-residence) to discover all things arboreal at the BSO's (https://www.bso.org) summer home. Between he and senior publicist Matthew Erikson (https://www.bso.org/about/staff-leadership/staff) we get a full view of the symphony of trees on their grounds, and their efforts to keep them in harmony.

episodes.title: A swift june powwow
episodes.text: We're starting the week with a flurry of activity. That activity includes veritable rock legends, as we talk with June Millington (https://www.ima.org/playlikeagirl.info/solo.html) about her band, Fanny (https://fannyrocks.com/) . They've got a documentary that's airing on PBS+ (https://www.pbs.org/show/fanny-right-rock/) and covers all the boundaries that the still performing and touring band broke over their tenure. That's not all though, as proven by IMA: The Institute for the Musical Arts (https://www.ima.org/about/) . We get into the origin stories of both the band and the hilltown organization helping folx rock hard. We encounter it in the 3rd Annual Odenong Powwow (https://www.facebook.com/OdenongPowwow) , which happens this weekend at Amherst-Pelham Regional High School. The event is free and open to the public, but is intentionally a space for especially Indigenous, Native, and First Nations people to convene and celebrate. Powwow singer, lecturer, and artist (https://www.artshubwma.org/profile/justin-beatty) Justin Beatty (https://www.jbdvart.com/) and organizer Kara Nye sat with us in studio to elaborate more on the events they have planned (including ways you can help (https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSf1Q3Hx8ln9Jmh7fFEnUS9Ain13y6uPCotaKRKEF6SV2fJu-A/viewform) make the whole thing happen (https://www.facebook.com/OdenongPowwow/posts/pfbid02ys5gxpf5StHy4NxkyNBrfEPcoU2BnjPqmRS4ZQtHsZTY1n7WvY6jxhYjxg1rWmmul) ). And we find it in the collisions on planets, as that's how moons get born sometimes. Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) , explains how looking at the craters on Mars' moons through successful rover missions (https://www.emiratesmarsmission.ae/) is giving folx a better understanding of how those celestial bodies come to be. And how, on occasion, those lunar blemishes accidentally ride the coattails of popstars.

episodes.title: Rose means bees
episodes.text: So much happening this weekend, let's dig in. First, we head back to Lenox to enter the Wine Thunderdome once again. Nejaime's Wine Cellars (https://www.nejaimeswine.com/) has set a pretty high precedent for these, becoming our first setting for a rosé showdown. And once again, Joe Nejaime has us doing our tasting blind, which is always a fun extra challenge. Then we skip across the mountains to discover that modern beekeeping has its roots in Franklin County. Beefest (https://www.greenfieldgbeefest.org) is now in its 13th year of celebrating (https://www.facebook.com/beefest) Greenfield's connection to our pollinating friends with art, demonstrations, and more. Organizer, and one of the founders of the festival, Sandy Thomas stops by to give us a rundown of the festival goings-on as well as a look at how reverend Lorenzo L. Langstroth's (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L._L._Langstroth) innovations still have impact today. And for Live music Friday, we bring in some big guns with Pamela Means (https://www.pamelameans.com/) , whose musical versatility simply cannot be contained. She's got a slew of gigs coming up all over New England (https://www.pamelameans.com/shows.html) , but you can often catch her at Luthier's Co-Op in Easthampton, where she's got a Jazz Residency happening (https://www.luthiers-coop.com/event/pamela-plus-one-3rd-thursday-pamela-means-residency-w-special-guest-7-830/2023-06-15/) every third Thursday. We dig into her musical history, wicked cool cover choices (https://www.instagram.com/pamelameansmusic/) , and her passion for justice and protest songs.

episodes.title: In flavor, in verse, in governance
episodes.text: Collaboration is the name of the game. We're pretty sure that Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) wishes that word was a little more prevalent in his goings-on Capitol Hill-wise. But debt ceiling (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-05-02/neal-calls-for-debt-ceiling-deal) and resignation (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-05-16/massachusetts-us-attorney-rachael-rollins-to-resign-after-justice-department-watchdog-probe) woes aside, the congressman himself has been up to a lot of working with other folx, including using a little technology to aid in a recent commencement speech (https://thisweekinworcester.com/mgovern-wpi-commencement-2023/) . Collaboration is at the very core of singing with other people, and we hear it in regional champions, all-female barbershop quartet Saffron (https://www.facebook.com/saffronquartet) . The four women who make up the group, Valerie Reynolds, Amanda Fowski, Sue Berry, and Christine Bulgini, stopped by the studios after teaching a master class on the genre to some of Marcos Carreras' (https://www.nepm.org/jazz-world/2021-03-18/marcos-carreras) students at Springfield Conservatory of the Arts (https://www.springfieldconservatoryofthearts.com/) . Saffron competes in the Northeast Regional Sweet Adeline's Competition (http://www.sai-region1.org/dbpage.php?pg=view&dbase=events&id=154781) on May19th, seeking to regain their title. And working together although your events are very far apart is what we got two former poet laureates to do. Martín Espada (https://www.martinespada.net) and Rich Michaelson (https://www.richardmichelson.com/) will each be giving readings on May 20th: Espada at a benefit for the LAVA center (https://thelavacenter.org/events/martin-espada-reading-book-signing-to-benefit-the-lava-center/) , and Michaelson at Forbes Library (https://forbeslibrary.libcal.com/event/10275621?hs=a) (with Michaelson giving a bonus reading through Zoom on May 21st (https://mailchi.mp/6815143e0235/csp-zoom-programs-week-of-april-5-5926124) ). In addition to hearing some of their new work, we also get a glimpse of the neighborhood in which they both grew up.

episodes.title: Strong Niagra Accessibility
episodes.text: There is a certain strength in demanding what is needed. We find this in the civil rights movement. In particular, a smaller predecessor to the movements we know now and came from the meeting of two Massachusetts minds is highlighted in an upcoming documentary. Florence filmmaker Lawrence "Larry" Hott (https://florentinefilms.org/about.html) joins us to preview his latest film titled "The Niagra Movement: The Early Battle for Civil Rights", which you can also see this weekend at the Northampton Center for the Arts (https://www.nohoarts.org/events/the-niagara-movement) just ahead of its debut on PBS+. He brings with him professor Aldon Morris (https://aldondmorris.com/) , who is featured in the film, to elaborate on the impact of the group's brief bright flame. It is also found in our ongoing struggle to bridge digital divides. May 18th is Global Accessibility Awareness Day (or GAAD) (https://accessibility.day/) which seeks to highlight and address issues and solutions for inclusion for folx with disabilities/impairments. Shivaji Kumar, (https://www.linkedin.com/in/shivaji-kumar-11aa7335) accessibility specialist, spoke to us about his personal experiences with technology as a blind person, and about some of the initiatives happening everywhere (https://gaad.foundation/) . And we find it as language evolves. The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) , senior editor at Merriam-Webster (https://www.merriam-webster.com/) , teaches us about strong verbs and how we as a language are slowly moving away from them, except for one particular word which seems to have snuck it's way into being one.

episodes.title: Immigrant birds in the hilltowns
episodes.text: Everyday is a good day to build more community. We forge connections between nature and humans, while watching what became of the dinosaurs, listening eagerly to their scintillating songs. David Sibley (https://www.sibleyguides.com/) , ornithologist, author, and artist, joins us to speak on all things birds, just as he will on May 22nd at a fundraiser for Tilton Library (https://www.eventbrite.com/e/david-sibley-at-tree-house-brewing-company-tickets-621723149677) . All this orchestrated by librarian Candice Bradbury (https://www.tiltonlibrary.org/staff/) , who tells us how this will all help the library's renovation plans. We also dig into connections forged while growing into a new home. The Center for New Americans (https://cnam.org/) helps immigrants in the area adapt and adjust to life stateside, and they'll be presenting "Immigrant Voices" at the Shea Theater this Sunday (https://sheatheater.org/d/14899/Center-for-New-Americans-Presents:-Immigrant-Voices-) with some of the folx who have benefited from their programs. Director Laurie Millman and current student Roberto Loccident of Haiti give us a glimpse of what the organization does and will show us onstage. And we reconnect with our neighbors, especially those in need. Hunt Chase (https://www.hilltowncdc.org/hunt-chase) of the Hilltown CDC (https://www.hilltowncdc.org/) came to our studios to chat about all the things the organization does for all the folx in the hidden hills, especially the Hilltown Mobile Market (https://hilltown-cdc.myshopify.com/) , which has seen remarkable growth and is incredibly useful to those who might otherwise have to go without.

episodes.title: Systems of plowshares to pizza
episodes.text: Today, we stumbled into turning points. Seems just about right for a Monday. There's the point at which a star looks at the planets around it and fails to recognize itself in the mirror. And we in turn, or at least those of us studying that star, fail to recognize it as well. This is the case with Fomalhaut (https://www.nasa.gov/feature/goddard/2023/webb-looks-for-fomalhaut-s-asteroid-belt-and-finds-much-more) , a star once likened to "The Eye of Sauron", but which is now being seen in a different light, and we sit with Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) , to discover why that is. There is the moment one realizes that the fight you're in may be the wrong one and you make a change. There are folx who bring forges to engender that change. Inspired by a passage in the Old Testament, Swords to Plowshares (https://www.s2pnortheast.org/) takes voluntarily surrendered firearms and converts them to usable garden tools using classic blacksmithing techniques. We chat with the head of the region's chapter of this organization, retired bishop Jim Curry (https://www.s2pnortheast.org/new-page-3) , and the person helping to bring him and his forge to Southampton this weekend (https://www.sneucc.org/eventdetail/17399692) , Sue Teece. And there is the instance where dough, cheese and sauce are not necessarily enough. Thus we embark on Pizza Quest IV at Pizzeria Boema in Lenox (https://pizzeriaboema.com/) , where we stray a little off of our regular path, to delicious results

episodes.title: Interconnected guitars and oyster wine
episodes.text: Weekends are for spreading out and connecting. There's connections to be found among the many musicians that make up the Happy Valley Guitar Orchestra (https://www.facebook.com/HappyValleyGuitarOrchestra/) . Originally founded by Peter Blanchette (https://archguitar.com/) , the collection of players combines now under the direction of Joseph Ricker. (https://www.facebook.com/joseph.ricker.52/) For live music Friday, we crashed their practice to hear them prepare for their spring concert "800 years" (https://www.nohoarts.org/events/category/happy-valley-guitar-orchestra-presents-eight-hundred-years) , which will encompass a wide swath of pieces within that span of time, including work from Grieg, Stereolab, and the artistic director himself. Then we go even further to bring musicians together. UMass Professor Felipe Salles (https://www.sallesjazz.com/) has just released an album new album called "Home Is Here" (https://caprirecords.com/html_index.cfm?page=item_page&itemoid=114518&itemname=-Felipe-Salles--Home-Is-Here) , featuring the Interconnected Ensemble, a whopping 19 member big band which creates multi-diasporic jazz. His inspiration is rooted in the nature of immigrant experiences, and we dig into his work as the ensemble get ready to present this album live in June (https://bombyx.live/events/felipe-salles-homeishere/) . And once again, we enter the wine thunderdome, pitting two wines against each other in the deep reaches under State Street Fruit Store (https://statestreetfruit.com/wine-beer-spirits/) in Northampton, seeking which vintage connects best with one of our favorite mollusks: oysters.

episodes.title: Collisions
episodes.text: Ideologies and worlds collide on today's show. First, our weekly check-in with our congressperson as we mcgovern with Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) . There's a lot happening on capital hill and beyond and we get his take on Santos' charges, the expiration of title 42, and more. Then we head to Florence where worlds will meet sonically this weekend. Colliderfest (https://bombyx.live/collider-fest) is coming to Bombyx (https://bombyx.live) this weekend and bringing with it musicians from all over the world and the sounds of their dance parties with them, including joropo, gqom (or igqom), Ethiopian groove, afrobeat, rumba, and more. Curator Edo Mor (https://www.momoglass.com) joins us to talk about the importance of keeping one's ears open to new musical experiences, all of the extra non-music things you can do at Colliderfest, and about a new concert experience (https://www.secretplanet.live) he's developing to have even more of these musicians visit the area in a more sustainable fashion. Plus Monte and Kaliis get a response to yesterday's conversation about "field", discuss a recent article about issues in an Amherst middle school, and introduce the concept of the "plywood ramp to the weekend". Author's Note: Since this episode was released, Colliderfest has since been canceled. No details of why have been made public, but more information about the artists who were coming is still available on the Bombyx website.

episodes.title: The words besieged
episodes.text: Today, we dive head first into local, and then national controversy surrounding one of our favorite things: word usage. There's buzz around the word "ladies" (https://www.nepm.org/2023-04-18/is-ladies-acceptable-workplace-language) and how it impacted the search for a new school superintendent in Easthampton (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-04-27/easthampton-school-board-reboots-superintendent-search-after-hiring-controversy) . Having heard from the mayor recently, we engage another side of this story: that of the word user himself, Vito Perrone, and get a different perspective on that situation and the impact of its aftermath. Then we sit with resident wordster, word nerd and senior editor at Merriam-Webster, Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) to discuss the word "field", which was recently removed from the language of an office at Smith College (https://www.gazettenet.com/Smith-College-School-of-Social-Work-ceases-use-of-the-word-field-50863290) . And law professor at Western New England University (https://www1.wne.edu/) , Jennifer Taub (https://jennifertaub.com) joins us and delves into the differences and distinctions between the two defamation cases Trump is embroiled in with E. Jean Carroll, including the one he just (https://www.nepm.org/2023-05-09/jury-finds-trump-liable-for-battery-defamation-in-e-jean-carroll-case) lost. (https://jentaub.substack.com/p/trump-town-hall-was-a-disaster)

episodes.title: Our Family Orchestra
episodes.text: With the massive attrition (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2018-08-07/letting-hay-rot-in-a-failing-dairy-farm-economy) of dairy farms in Massachusetts (https://www.barstowslongviewfarm.com/summary-of-the-2018-dairy-crisis-a-ma-dairy-farmer-perspective/) , (and the alarming amount of sad cow stories we've had on the show), we're turning our attention to a co-op that is still making milk magic happen. Angie Facey of Bree-z Knoll Farm (https://www.facebook.com/breezknollfarmllc/) , which is a part of Our Family Farms (https://ourfamilyfarms.com) , shows us around their new facilities, including a robot that uses lasers to milk their cows, and a brand new, only-been-open-for-a-month milk processing plant. Then we head east to encounter a group determined to make everyone feel closer to symphonic music. The New England Repertory Orchestra (https://www.nerorchestra.org) , or NERO, is a boundary-breaking, canon-rethinking, community-entrenched organization that does that and more. Presenting a pair of works centering grief, including a premiere of a newly commissioned piece from Felix Jarrar (https://www.felixjarrarmusic.com/) , the orchestra takes the stage at The Shea Theater (https://sheatheater.org/d/14896/New-England-Repertory-Orchestra-Presents:-BANISHING-GRIEF) this weekend. We chat with artistic director and conductor Cailin Marcel Manson (https://www.cailinmarcelmanson.com/) and violin first-chair Michi Wiancko (https://www.michiwiancko.com/) about how diversity as a mantra helps them change the landscape and equity of symphonic and operatic music.

episodes.title: Prideful films of a hungry sun
episodes.text: We're starting the week off with a bang! And that's literal in the case of the sun. Just in time for all your existential dread, Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) , talks to us about how the sun will eventually devour the Earth. The process is genuinely cool and we found out more about how this happens by watching another star eat one of its planets (https://news.mit.edu/2023/astronomers-spot-star-swallowing-planet-0503) . For a good portion of May, that bang will bring a plethora of films of various shapes, genres, and sizes to the valley. The Easthampton Film Festival (https://www.easthamptonfilmfestival.com/) sees its second year and a huge expansion of programing to go along with that. Organizer Chris Ferry joined us in studio to chat about all the things you can see. He brought along Wally Marzano-Lesnevich (http://www.wallyml.com/) and Melissa Dimetres (https://www.melissadimetres.com/) , both local filmmakers who will be showing work over the course of the festival. And Monte went to Hampshire Pride this weekend, where he got to ask mayor Gina-Louise Sciarra (https://www.northamptonma.gov/720/Mayors-Office) , councilors Garrick Perry (https://www.garrickperryward4.com/) and Marissa Elkins (https://www.marissaelkins.com/) , and police chief Jodi Kasper (https://www.northamptonpd.com/about/meet-the-chief.html) their feelings on the return of the festival. He also captured the proclamation that might have been hinted at previously. (https://www.nepm.org/podcast/the-fabulous-413/2023-05-02/pride-and-progress)

episodes.title: A young mezcal is never dusty
episodes.text: Music, mezcal, and OSHA are what we're all about today! Don't think these things go together? Well, we're about to be the center of that particular Venn diagram. You may have heard about a death due to dust inhalation at a dispensary in Holyoke (https://www.gazettenet.com/Holyoke-cannabis-reaches-OSHA-agreement-49348540) . We bring in reporter and UMass Professor Dusty Christensen (https://www.umass.edu/journalism/facultyStaff/bio/christensen) , who recently wrote a more in depth article about this incident and other related issues for The Nation (https://www.thenation.com/article/society/cannabis-industry-worker-safety/) . We pick his brain about the inequities of cannabis production. One vice deserves another. So in honor of Cinco de Mayo ( a distinctly Texan holiday, we learned (https://www.nepm.org/podcast/the-fabulous-413/2023-05-05/may-the-cinco-plates-be-with-you) ) we enter the Mezcal Thunderdome with local chef and foodways activist Neftalí Durán (https://www.icollectiveinc.org/who-we-are) , who lets us taste 2 very different mezcals straight from Oaxaca. He also lets us know how we all could be doing mezcal better (https://www.instagram.com/p/CXt9HVEPjEg/?igshid=YmMyMTA2M2Y%3D) . And since rock comes in all shapes, sizes, and ages, we get down with a few members of the infamous Young at Heart Chorus (https://youngatheartchorus.com/) , who are putting a performance on this Sunday at the Academy of Music called "The Love Show" (https://www.aomtheatre.com/event/young-at-heart-chorus-2023/) . They are always a delight, even ask us to sing along! Who are we to refuse a request for backup singers?

episodes.title: May the cinco plates be with you
episodes.text: It is wars in the stars day! Which means it's the day before Cinco de Mayo, often celebrated in ways that are... shall we say questionable. But Latinas 413 (https://www.instagram.com/latinas413/?hl=en) and Hot Plate Brewing (https://www.hotplatebeer.com) are seeking to change all that, while still having fun in the process. They're hosting a Cinco de Mayo event (https://www.berkshireeagle.com/news/local/hot-plate-brewing-company-to-host-cinco-de-mayo-event/article_b64f6d2c-e7b1-11ed-9443-bb02573b54bd.html) at the brewery that day and we checked in with Sarah Real (https://www.instagram.com/hotplatebeer/) of Hot Plate, and Lilliana Atanacio, Perla Mendoza, and Laura Cabrera (https://www.instagram.com/yo_soy_arte/) of Latinas 413 to hear more about the actual history of the holiday as well as what those festivities are going to entail. We mcgovern with Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) about the country's Cuba policies and his trepidation surrounding the news of the possible Umass Amherst Layoffs (https://www.masslive.com/news/2023/05/umass-amherst-prepares-to-issue-up-to-124-layoff-notices-amid-privatization-plan.html) . But we also asked our Congressperson what his favorite Star Wars® character is, because it's May the fourth, and we are nerds at our core. We want to find out who else in the NEPM building is feeling the force, so we ask our colleagues about the saga that continues.

episodes.title: Informal transgender comics
episodes.text: It's our 50th episode! Time to party! And we're not the only ones. Transhealth (https://transhealth.org/) , an all queer run organization seeking equity and unity in gender care, is celebrating too! And although their official 2nd anniversary show is sold out (https://bombyx.live/events/transhealth-birthday-benefit/) , that doesn't mean that we can't party with them. CEO Dallas Ducar (https://www.dallasducar.com/) and development director Em Ayers swing through to let us know what the organization has gained so far and what they have on the horizon. Getting free things is totally a festivity and Free Comic Book Day (https://www.freecomicbookday.com/) is happening on May 6th, so we pestered Christian W.Z. Reader of Comics 'N More (https://www.comicsmongers.com/) in Easthampton about their store's involvement with the event (https://www.facebook.com/events/459597852994450/?acontext=%7B%22ref%22%3A%2252%22%2C%22action_history%22%3A%22%5B%7B%5C%22surface%5C%22%3A%5C%22share_link%5C%22%2C%5C%22mechanism%5C%22%3A%5C%22share_link%5C%22%2C%5C%22extra_data%5C%22%3A%7B%5C%22invite_link_id%5C%22%3A824270211965193%7D%7D%5D%22%7D) as well as the innovative ways they've figured out to get even more folx into comics. And we can't be formal at parties, not the fun ones anyhow. The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) senior editor at Merriam-Webster talks with our hosts about the casualizing of language: both spoken and written.

episodes.title: Pride and Progress
episodes.text: This weekend is Pride in Hampshire County! Yes, a whole month before the rest of the country! But Northampton Pride gets its start under much smaller and more dangerous circumstances. Dr. Jane Fleischman (https://www.janefleishman.com/meetjane) was there at the first few marches, back when it was the Gay and Lesbian Liberation March. Although she primarily digs into sexuality of folx later in life with her practice Speaking Of, LLC and her podcast Our Better Half (https://www.ourbetterhalf.net) , we speak with her about those beginnings and its initial progress. Once we examine the origins, we turn to its future as Hampshire Pride (https://linktr.ee/hampshire.pride?fbclid=IwAR2Pgi9LaTck9nui0ngKxxktdeIz-S-32m8tgmkIxNEpDf4ir7P9zi4ZFz8) emerges phoenix-like from the ashes of its Northampton predecessor. Organizers Clay Pearson, Kayla Abney (https://www.majesticsaloon.com/) and Celina Almendarez join us to talk about the importance of preserving not just the Pride March itself, but queer spaces in general. They are just fantastically decked out in our studio and we have the pics to prove it.

episodes.title: The windy planetarium near Whoville
episodes.text: It is more than just our mornings that become eclectic this Monday. We bring another installment of "Meet the Mayor", this time heading up I-91 to Easthampton to chat with Nicole LaChapelle (https://www.nicolelachapelle.com/) . A lot has happened in that city as of late, so we talk about it and her love of local music. Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) , comes with us to the re-opening of the Seymour Planetarium at Springfield Museums (https://springfieldmuseums.org/about/springfield-science-museum/seymour-planetarium/#) this past weekend. We get his thoughts on the update as well as some of their other exhibits! And Julie Lemieux of Windy Ridge Organics (https://windyridgeorganics.com) in Hawley stops through to speak about their interesting and innovative CSA program, and how they raise things off the farm as well!

episodes.title: Double the wine, triple the music
episodes.text: With all of the stress of the week, we figured we would not only double but triple down on the relaxing today. We head to West Springfield for the Wine Thunderdome once again. It's Sardenga versus Tuscany as Michael Quinlan (https://blog.tableandvine.com/contact/about/) of Table & Vine (https://tableandvine.com) brings us two Vermentinos to try out. Just ahead of their show at the Academy of Music, Langhorne Slim (https://langhorneslimmusic.com/) and John Craigie (https://johncraigiemusic.com/) join us in studio. Each has a new album out, and although their songwriting styles seem very different from each other, their partnership dovetails them well, and as does their love for both The Beatles and the late Harry Belafonte. And pillar of the local rock scene, and endless font of stories about said scene, Ray Mason (https://www.raymason.com/) pays us a visit. He's got a new album (https://raymason.bandcamp.com/) coming out soon called "Is There Wiggle Room?" about which we are quite excited.

episodes.title: Revitalizing the daily hip-hop budgets
episodes.text: If you had no plans for this weekend, no worries, we've got you covered. There's so much happening in the 413 this weekend that we wish there were more hours in the day. A lot of those happenings involve hip-hop, where a legend in the genre is making an appearance in Easthampton this weekend. Large Professor (https://www.instagram.com/xplargepro/?hl=en) performs at Daily Operation on Saturday (https://www.eatdailyop.com/live-music/large-professor-with-special-guests) and we chat with chef/owner David Schrier about how that's just one small part of keeping hip-hop vibrant in Western Mass. From there we go to Bombyx (https://bombyx.live/) in Florence, where the 2nd Annual Power of Truths Festival (https://bombyx.live/power-of-truths/) is taking place on April 29th & 30th. The innovative and ambitious program seeks to inspire folx in order to create more equitable communities, and we chat with Michael Lawrence-Riddell of Self-Evident Education (https://selfevidenteducation.com/) and Jared Bridgeman, aka Akrobatik (https://akrobatikmc.bandcamp.com/music) about how Hip Hop is a large part of that picture and American history as a whole. This Saturday will also see the return of GreenNFit (https://www.revitalizecdc.com/greennfit) , a volunteer driven event heralded by Revitalize Springfield (a subset of Revitalize CDC (https://www.revitalizecdc.com/) ). We talk with President & CEO Colleen Shanley-Loveless and Director of Programs Ethel Griffin about the impact of this program and others throughout the Connecticut River Valley. And we mcgovern with Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) , who is encountering and enduring difficult terrain on Capitol Hill.

episodes.title: The routes of word and poets
episodes.text: We hope you like language because we're getting deep into it today. The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster, (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) senior editor at Merriam-Webster (https://merriam-webster.com/) here in Springfield pulls back the curtain to show the bones of how definitions actually work. Former poet laureate, and current professor at UMass, Karen Skolfield (https://karenskolfield.com) celebrates National Poetry Month with us by sharing some of her experiences and a bit of her work (https://karenskolfield.com/poems/) as well. At Smith College a production of La Ruta (https://www.smith.edu/story/about-smith/performing-arts/la-ruta) is running this weekend. The show examines the lives of women working and living around Juárez, and director, and new faculty member, Monica Lopez Orozco (https://www.smith.edu/academics/faculty/monica-lopez-orozco) and one of the actors in the piece, Naidelyn Cruz (https://www.backstage.com/u/naidelyn-cruz/) join us to talk about the import and impact of the play. And tomorrow is McGoverning with Rep. Jim McGovern! (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) Email your questions for the congressman to fab413@nepm.org (mailto:fab413@nepm.org) or text us at [1-800-639-9120](tel:18006399120).

episodes.title: Saving Moon Tongued Music
episodes.text: We got up to music, and farms, and moons today, oh my! Damany Gordon of Genuine Culture LLC (https://genuineculturellc.com) in Holyoke is heralding a night of classic hip-hop in Springfield at White Lion Brewing (https://whitelionbrewing.com/) this upcoming weekend. It honors the collective Native Tongues (https://allevents.in/springfield/its-saturday-a-native-tongues-tribute/200024313426250) and we tap into why that group is so important and influential. We hear from Chelsea Gazillo of the American Farmland Trust (http://www.farmland.org) about the Bay State's efforts to save small farms, as well as a preview of the story she'll be sharing as part of Field Notes (https://www.aomtheatre.com/event/field-notes-an-afternoon-of-storytelling/) , CISA's storytelling event happening this weekend. NEPM reporter Jill Kaufman (https://www.nepm.org/people/jill-kaufman) keys us into a progressive and effective music therapy program (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-04-25/a-lot-of-bang-for-your-buck-with-jail-based-arts-programs) happening in Franklin County. It's akin to other programs across the country seeing similar results and involving some big names as well. And Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) , shows us insights and connections between the moon, math, and Eid as Ramadan nears it's end.

episodes.title: Making Earth Codes
episodes.text: Today was all about being both collegiate and past tense. First, Umass had a Bach Festival (https://blogs.umass.edu/bach/) this weekend! As a part of the festivities, the Asian and Asian American Studies Arts and Culture Center brought three prominent composers and performers to campus to perform new works: Hyeyung Sol Yoon (https://www.hyeyung.com/) , Texu Kim (https://www.texukim.com/) , and Vijay Iyer (https://vijay-iyer.com/) . We talk with them about issues relating to western canon and how moving on from that influences their work now. And since climate change is very very real, and officially Earth day was this past Saturday (though an argument could be made that every day is Earth Day), we sat down with some folx from the Five College area to check in on what their particular campuses are doing regarding sustainability. It was a crowded house with Beth Hooker from Smith College (https://www.smith.edu/about-smith/sustainable-smith) , Sara Draper from Hampshire College (https://www.hampshire.edu/hampshire-experience/sustainable-hampshire) , Ezra Small from UMass Amherst (https://www.umass.edu/sustainability/) , and Weston Dripps from Amherst College (https://www.amherst.edu/about/sustainability/office-of-sustainability) (one day we'll catch them all). Each of their sustainability initiatives is gaining ground, and we got to hear about how they're collaborating with other colleges and universities in the Bay State (https://farmingtonsolarproject.weebly.com/necrp.html) to do even better.

episodes.title: A shot is a nuclear cocktail
episodes.text: When you live close enough to someone who's won multiple Nobel prizes and the Gandhi/King/Ikeda (https://masspeaceaction.org/ira-helfand-wins-gandhi-king-ikeda-award/) prize from Morehouse College, you have to get them in studio with you. You just have to do that. That is how we end up talking with Dr. Ira Helfand about the threat of nuclear war and all the things we can do to stop it, including the mission of the Back from the Brink Campaign (https://preventnuclearwar.org/) . And that was such heavy conversation for the first day of the weekend that we needed to lighten our moods a little. Introducing: The Cocktail Thunderdome. Sean Billson of Judd's Restaurant (https://www.gatewaycityarts.com/food/judds-restaurant/) at Gateway City Arts (https://www.gatewaycityarts.com) in Holyoke, who pits two variations of Corpse Reviver no. 2 against each other. And it's live music Friday, which we dig into with Noam Schatz (https://www.facebook.com/HuntersHeartMushrooms) of The Lucky Shots (https://www.luckyshotsmusic.com/) who have a new album (https://theluckyshots.bandcamp.com/album/count-to-nothing) that's just come out and a show on April 29th to celebrate that.

episodes.title: Hemp nation under a groove
episodes.text: It is the 20th day of April, which is celebrated by many it turns out. But being in one of the few states with legal access to jazz cabbage is not without its nuances too. Payton Shubrick (https://businesswest.com/blog/payton-shubrick-sets-out-to-build-a-family-success-story/?utm_source=ourcommunitynow&utm_medium=web) of 6 Bricks (https://6bricks.com/about/) dispensary in Springfield, one of only 3 black woman owned in the whole state, chats with us to dig into some of the issues with equity and equality in the industry. (She also shares our fascination with antiquated terminology for cannabis.) Then we bring a pile of young actors from First Generation (https://performanceproject.org/projects/first-generation.html) into the studio to give us a taste of Shout! Elevate! Inspire! (https://performanceproject.org/blog/78-shout-elevate-inspire-benefit-concert-2023.html) : a performance they'll be a part of at Springfield Technical Community College (https://www.stcc.edu/) this weekend alongside Samira Evans, Haneef Nelson and more. Julie Lichtenberg and James Arana of The Performance Project (https://performanceproject.org) also let us know more places you can catch the show they excerpted. And after a week's hiatus, we are once again back to mcgoverning with Representative Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) , who shares his worries about guns, transgender kids, and other issues arising on Capitol Hill.

episodes.title: Retro runways to tumble upon
episodes.text: We're hoping to fit in with the cool kids today. Some of those cool kids are running on airport runways.
