We talk to Julie Kumble of Treehouse Foundation (https://www.treehousefoundation.net/) and Holly Lurgio of the Northampton Airport (https://northamptonairport.com/) about the Runway 5k (https://www.pledgereg.com/runway-5k) happening on April 23rd, where they'll be raising funds by letting people run where the airplanes roam (but not while the airplanes roam). We find out how Treehouse is changing the landscape of foster care and that yes, some folx do build their planes from kits. And other cool kids will be hanging out with Lindsay Patterson and Marshall Escamilla from the Tumble Science Podcast for Kids (https://www.sciencepodcastforkids.com/) , who in addition to bringing their songs and knowledge to Meltdown in Greenfield (https://www.hawksandreed.com/event/the-rivers-family-music-meltdown-and-book-bash/) this weekend, bring us tales about birds and blind scientists. But the coolest kids of all can go backwards in time with their words. Resident wordster, Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) of Merriam-Webster (https://www.merriam-webster.com/) teaches us about a term coined by former NPR President Frank Mankiewicz and how it dates the evolution of technology.

episodes.title: A noted field of grief and pie
episodes.text: Some stories are best told by the people who have actually lived them. But we can accompany those stories with pizza, because food is wonderful. Pizza Quest volume 3 hits the Berkshires as we make a stop, sadly without our amateur pizziaolo, to Betty's Pizza Shack (https://bettyspizza.com/) in Lenox. Then, as it is still April and therefore still National Poetry Month, local poet and social worker Lauren Singer (https://www.instagram.com/laurenoscopy/) joins us with some of her work and updates on her many projects examining grief and adaptations to loss, including a new podcast. And CISA (https://www.buylocalfood.org/) is preparing to host Field Notes, stories of our local foodways, at the Academy of Music (https://www.aomtheatre.com/event/field-notes-an-afternoon-of-storytelling/) . So we get a small preview of what will be onstage April 30th with Jacob Nelson and Trouble Erin Anne Gouch Mandeson. **Please note: The Lauren Singer readings mention child death.

episodes.title: Sunshine juice runs
episodes.text: We get to go both forward and backward in time today, which is fitting for a Monday somehow. Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) tells us about the ESA's new probe JUICE ( already taking selfies in space (https://www.esa.int/Science_Exploration/Space_Science/Juice/Juice_sends_first_selfies_from_space) ), its mission to the moons of Jupiter, and the lengths we do to make sure the probe looks but doesn't touch. Then we digress quite a bit about pizza and semantics with Jarrett J. Krosoczka (https://www.studiojjk.com/) . He's got a new graphic memoir out called "Sunshine" (https://shop.scholastic.com/parent-ecommerce/books/sunshine-9781338356311.html) that will receive a full cast reading treatment on April 18th at the Academy of Music in Northampton (https://www.aomtheatre.com/event/sunshine-live-unabridged/) . We pick his brain about the origins of the book and the ways the events have influenced his life today. Plus it was the holiday that only 5 other states celebrate (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patriots%27_Day) , and on which we look east to watch folx run 26.2 miles into the state capital (https://www.baa.org/races/boston-marathon) . Neither of our hosts are particularly fond of running, however, so they look to you for other marathon-esque activities you may have participated in! (Do tell us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) , we are curious indeed)

episodes.title: Sleepy Winter Wine
episodes.text: It was wicked warm out today. But just because it's warming up, doesn't mean we can't get comfy with it. The heat makes us think of white wine. In the Berkshires we have our very first set of white wines in the thunderdome, courtesy of Nejaime's Wine Cellars (https://nejaimeswine.com/) in Lenox. Our hosts Joe Nejaime and Xavier Letteron even threw an extra wrench into this showdown, for which we were entirely game. Our studios are currently being moved down the street to our new offices, but it's Friday, and having live music has become a fixture. So instead we headed to Northampton to meet Flora Reed (https://secondhandsongs.com/artist/22256) and Philip Price (https://www.philipbprice.com/) of The Winterpills (https://www.winterpills.com/) to do a live music Friday at The Parlor Room instead. With beverages and music covered, we just need a good read to round it out. Lucky for us, local author and Pulitzer Prize winner Tracy Kidder (https://www.tracykidder.com/) has a new book out called "Rough Sleepers: Dr. Jim O'Connell's Urgent Mission to Bring Healing to Homeless People" (https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/594500/rough-sleepers-by-tracy-kidder/) . We chat with him about the people called to help, and how simple solutions don’t always suit complex problems. Cozy bases all covered. **Please note: The reading included from the Tracy Kidder book mentions suicidal ideation

episodes.title: Circle of music and resistance
episodes.text: We're preserving and creating community today. We find out about a decades strong jazz jam session (https://www.thedrakeamherst.org/events/rej2nz35h5d8n42lkglls5xehpwg7n) in Amherst being revitalized through the interest of more young people attending. Reporter Ben James (https://www.benjabirdy.com/) takes us to The Drake (https://www.thedrakeamherst.org/) to see what's going down. A group of 5th and 6th graders at Fort River School (https://arps.org/fr/) are just now learning how to change the world through their civic engagement class. They walk us through their projects with a little help from their teacher, Tim Austin. And one of those projects relates to educator and member of North-South America Chithue (https://tibetanparliament.org) (the Tibetan Parliament in Exile) Thondup Tsering (https://thonduptsering.com/) , who is helping to bring activist and poet Tenzin Tsundue (https://www.tenzintsundue.com/) to speak on UMass campus on April 17th (https://www.umass.edu/events/talk-writing-and-resistance-tenzin-tsundue) about the release of his latest book, "ནང། Nowhere to Call Home". Together they paint a bigger picture of the importance of resistance, and the connections that resilience builds. And Greenfield drummer, drum tech for The Rolling Stones, and general awesome person in sound Don McAulay (https://www.moderndrummer.com/article/don-mcaulay-charlie-watts-drum-tech/) stopped in to help us celebrate the life of a pillar of the local music community, Stone Coyote's (https://stonecoyotes.com/) drummer Doug Tibbles (https://dougtibblesart.com/) , who we lost earlier this week.

episodes.title: Spelt opposition
episodes.text: It's fun when you discover things a little closer to home than you first thought. It's true of language and turns out it's true of grain too. Word Nerd Emily Brewster (https://www.nepm.org/people/emily-brewster) chats with us about prefixed words whose opposites pre-date their root word. We are quite gruntled to know there's a running list of them. Then we skip across the river to take a look at what was for quite some time the only malting house east of the Mississippi. Andrea and Christian Stanley of Valley Malt (https://www.valleymalt.com/) and Ground Up Grain (https://www.groundupgrain.com) moved their operation from Hadley to Holyoke, upgrading some of their equipment and products while they did it. Along with Phil Korman of CISA (https://www.buylocalfood.org/about/people/staff/) , we stopped into their new digs to take a tour, hear how their plans and business have evolved over the past decade and change, and eat goodies brought by their baking consultant: Deb Bernardini (https://www.groundupgrain.com/blog/meet-micro-bakery-forno-413) .

episodes.title: The words of way finders
episodes.text: Let's take a look at houses. We dig into housing equity from more sides than one with H. John Fisher, fair housing manager for Way Finders (https://www.wayfinders.org/) in Springfield. Their 17th annual Fair Housing and Civil Rights Conference (https://fhcrconference.com/) which will continue through the 13th, so we ask about some of the problems we can easily see, which of course leads to conversations about the ones we can't. Then we segue into poetry with someone who used to be a tenant lawyer. Martín Espada (http://www.martinespada.net/) , poet and professor of English at UMass Amherst, joins us to chat about his work (http://www.martinespada.net/floaters.html) , it's connection to community, and the language that joins us all. He'll be reading at Greenfield Community College (https://www.gcc.mass.edu/events/poetry-reading-martin-espada/) on April 12th, so we asked him to read for us too. (He obliged.) And just in case you maybe have a burning question for Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) , here's a quick reminder that you can send us your questions for him anytime! Just email thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) , or text us at [1-800-639-9120](tel:18006399120), and we'll ask the congressman your query!

episodes.title: The history of rogue poetry
episodes.text: Mondays need a little beauty in them, and as we find ourselves right in the middle of National Poetry Month, it seems fitting to bring in a little lyricism to allay our daily doldrums. So we talk to one of the poet laureates of Easthampton, Jason Montgomery (https://www.attackbearpress.com/attackbears#/attack-bear-jason-montgomery) . Although he and his partner Alex Woolner will soon be stepping down from the position, that didn't stop us from talking about their various poetry (https://www.attackbearpress.com/projects#/pvm) projects (https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=892149892034743&set=a.746308779952189) at Attack Bear Press (https://www.attackbearpress.com) , and his art in and outside of the 50 Arrow Gallery (https://50arrowgallery.com) , plus a cool form of poetry (https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=884089762840756&set=pb.100037192227138.-2207520000.) they'll be highlighting on April 13th. We spoke to the meter of buildings and architecture with Dylan Gaffney of Forbes Library's Local History & Special Collections, who will present a series of photographs (https://forbeslibrary.libcal.com/event/9043225) documenting the evolving landscape of Northampton from 1887-1987. And we discovered the prose of space with Mr. Universe, Hampshire College's Salman Hameed, (https://www.hampshire.edu/academics/faculty/salman-hameed) who showed us the magic of rogue black holes.

episodes.title: Egg music and holy wine
episodes.text: It's Good Friday! Good as in we have a pile of wicked good things we get into. First, in a confrontation months in the making, we invite NEPM's own Elizabeth Román (https://www.nepm.org/people/elizabeth-roman) to live taste test UK vs USA Cadbury Creme Eggs. Is there a difference that you can really notice? And just in time for passover, we head to Provisions (https://provisionswine.com/) to have two kosher wines enter the thunderdome! Benson Hyde (https://www.instagram.com/thenatureofwine/?hl=en) and Bruce Amis let us sample some non-Manischewitz options available this holiday. Plus a musical double header! We catch band leader for Snarky Puppy (https://snarkypuppy.com/about) , Michael League (https://michaelleagueplaysmusic.com/) , on his way to sound check and ask him what's in store for their show at the UMass Fine Arts Center (https://fac.umass.edu/Online/default.asp?BOparam::WScontent::loadArticle::permalink=SnarkyPuppy&BOparam::WScontent::loadArticle::context_id=) next week. Then local bluegrass heroes Poor Monroe (http://www.poormonroebluegrass.com/) stop into our very stripped down studios ahead of their concert at Gateway City Arts (https://www.gatewaycityarts.com/event/poor-monroe/) on Saturday to share their musical histories and some aquavit. Good things for your Friday, indeed.

episodes.title: Passover robots and MDAR firsts
episodes.text: We bring extra springtime in the studio today, now that it's warm! We welcome Ashley Randle (https://www.mass.gov/service-details/about-mdar-commissioner) , the first female-identifying commissioner (https://www.gazettenet.com/South-Deerfield-native-named-ag-commissioner-50081660) of the Massachusetts Department of Agricultural Resources (MDAR). She tells about some of the department's plans, raising cows, and farming across the baystate both rural and urban. Then we get into the not-so-Jewish past of Springfield with editor and podcaster Mark Oppenheimer (https://www.markoppenheimer.com/) of the podcast Unorthodox (https://www.tabletmag.com/podcasts/unorthodox) . He also keys us into some of the new traditions folx might see in their Passover festivities. (Chag Sameach to those celebrating!) And Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) sidesteps the indictment debacle. With that much needed breath of fresh air, we instead learn how he's on the congressional robotics caucus advisory committee, that the robotics caucus exists (https://roboticscaucus.org/) , and how his work on that committee lead to a recent visit to Worcester Polytechnic Institute (https://www.wpi.edu/) . And then an alarming amount of Star Wars references happen. They may or may not be acronym related.

episodes.title: Don't let the Pigeon cook the books
episodes.text: We all need a bit of reprieve from Tuesday's kerfuffle. So we dig into some books instead! NYT bestselling author, opera composer, and Kennedy Center education artist in residence (https://www.kennedy-center.org/education/mo-willems/) , Mo Willems (https://pigeonpresents.com/) shows us his latest art exhibit, Wrecked Shapes. And we celebrate the 20th anniversary of perhaps his best known character: Pigeon. Then we head to Hatfield, where the largest collection of cookbooks resides! At least that's according to the Guinness Book of World Records (https://www.guinnessworldrecords.com/) , and its winner Lisa Ekus (https://ekusgroup.com/people/lisa-ekus/) let us run rampant among its tomes while telling us how the massive assortment came to be. Her daughter Sally Ekus (https://medium.com/authority-magazine/sally-ekus-of-the-ekus-group-on-how-to-grow-your-business-or-brand-by-writing-a-book-1cab1ff10b5b) also gave us insight to the cookbook publishing industry and their place in it. (https://ekusgroup.com/) Plus we hear some of your thoughts about the goings on, locally with the use of the word "Ladies" in Easthampton (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-04-05/meeting-on-easthampton-superintendent-abruptly-canceled-after-virtual-session-is-flooded) , and nationally with the aforementioned indictment (https://www.nepm.org/2023-03-31/trump-indictment-asking-legal-questions) ! Tell us yours at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or text us at [18006399120!](tel:18006399120)

episodes.title: The Moushabeck Moushabook Mashup
episodes.text: Hampshire College Astronomer Dr. Salman Hameed (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) on the James Webb's deep dive into the TRAPPIST-1 system. Is there a planet there that could host life as we know it? Plus the unveiling of the Artemis I crew. And Hannah Moushabeck (https://hannahmoushabeck.com/) on her new book Homeland: My Father Dreams of Palestine with special guests her father, Michel (https://www.interlinkbooks.com/brand/michel-moushabeck/) , and her uncle, Gabriel (https://booklinkbooks.com/about-us/) . All three of these Moushabecks have a lifelong dedication to books, bookstores and publishing. We'll hear about those connections and how they link up to Western Mass.

episodes.title: An indictment, an indigenous voice, two cloudy wines and Cloudbelly
episodes.text: Surprise! Former President Donald Trump was indicted yesterday, much to everyone's surprise. Western New England University Law Professor, Jen Taub (https://jennifertaub.com/) , give us her take on the news. Tasting the King and Queen of Northern Italian wines in The Wine Thunderdome with the Wine Snobs from State Street Fruit Store, Deli, Wines and Spirits (https://statestreetfruit.com/wine-beer-spirits/) in Northampton. Jennifer Lee, from the Narragansett Tribe, on the Indigenous Voices screening at The Lava Center in Greenfield (https://thelavacenter.org/events/indigenous-voices-video-series-screening-and-discussion/) . And the band Cloudbelly (https://www.cloudbellytheband.com/) , performing at The Drake in Amherst tonight.

episodes.title: McGovern on guns and the daily mass shootings
episodes.text: A conversation with Congressman Jim McGovern in the wake of the Nashville shooting. Will a Republican controlled Congress take any steps? And why was Richard Gere hugging the congressman on the steps of The Capitol? Then a tour of a new exhibit as Mass MoCA called " to see oneself at a distance, (https://massmoca.org/event/to-see-oneself-at-a-distance/) " with artists Suneil Sanzgiri and Tu Le. And an informal poll: Red Sox or Yankees? It's Opening Day form Major League Baseball. The 413 has divided allegiances. Where do your allegiances lie?

episodes.title: Fighting hunger gives me the Tingles
episodes.text: Comedian Jimmy Tingle and the FCCMP

episodes.title: Empty Bowls & Burrito Bowls
episodes.text: Monte & Kaliis talk with Phil Korman from CISA (https://www.buylocalfood.org/) and with Bob Lowry from Bueno y Sano (https://buenoysano.com/) about the history of that now iconic Western Mass Burrito shop. They even get a DoorDash delivery and a briefing about how DoorDash is impacting the restaurants and the communities the restaurants serve. And then a conversation with Lev BenEzra, executive director of The Amherst Survival Center (https://amherstsurvival.org/) about their upcoming Empty Bowls Dinner and about how a cut in pandemic era SNAP benefits is affecting the greater Amherst community.

episodes.title: Screens of literature and gender
episodes.text: This time we're bridging the gap between the page and screen. Big screens. Mr. Universe, Salman Hameed, puts on his other hat, that as a member of the directorial board for Amherst Cinema (https://amherstcinema.org/) , to inform us about their Science on Screen series (https://amherstcinema.org/series/science-screen) , starting with the film adaptation of "After Yang" (which is also a pretty great novella (https://bookshop.org/p/books/children-of-the-new-world-stories-alexander-weinstein/8481776?ean=9781250098993&gclid=CjwKCAjwoIqhBhAGEiwArXT7K2MQplh-eRc0Di9m9BZaZ4qo_lXTNcZPie6gZDLJFj2yH8rYWimImhoC_msQAvD_BwE) , if you're into reading it as well). We check in with NEPM Reporter Nirvani Williams (https://www.nepm.org/people/nirvani-williams) who spoke with Northampton locals Gwen Agna and Shelley Rotner, authors of a new book for children on gender, "True You: A Gender Journey" (https://www.harpercollins.com/products/true-you-gwen-agnashelley-rotner?variant=39913450799138) . And director/actor/screenwriter/novelist John Sayles (https://www.johnsaylesblog.com/) stops by to talk about his impressive body of work, including his new book "Jamie MacGillivray: The Renegade's Journey" (https://www.mhpbooks.com/books/jamie/) , which he'll be promoting at Odyssey Bookshop (https://www.odysseybks.com/) later this week.

episodes.title: Tabling barns and institutes
episodes.text: To paraphrase one of our guests, the mountains were very much the backbone of this show. Nestled in them, we found ourselves back at the Clark Art Institute (https://www.clarkart.edu/) with director of communications Vicki Saltzman, perusing some of their new acquisitions and finding out how they are celebrating the 50th birthday of their building (https://www.clarkart.edu/event/detail/2214-90277) . The mountains were a large factor behind the two regions of wines we sampled as we once again entered the Wine Thunderdome. This time led by Michael Quinlan (https://blog.tableandvine.com/contact/about/) of Table & Vine (https://tableandvine.com/) in West Springfield, we get to experience a bit of a play on the concept, and get a bonus opinion in the room! And in the studio, we brought music that you can hear in the Berkshires this weekend at their Saturday show at the Egremont Barn (https://www.theegremontbarn.com/) . Mike Stinson (https://mikestinsonmusic.com/) and Johnny Irion (https://www.johnnyirionmusic.com) swing by to regale us with a few selections from their brand new album ”Working My Way Down” (https://blackwingmusic.com/) . Correction: Within the show we refer to Egremont Barn as being in Stockbridge, which is in error. Egremont Barn is located in Egremont, MA.

episodes.title: Real Clark, fake judges
episodes.text: In our plywood ramp to the weekend, we dig into some more art. We head to Williamstown to tour the Clark Art Institute (https://www.clarkart.edu/) with director of communications Vicki Saltzman (https://www.visitma.com/blog/2012/05/ma-spotlight-on-vicki-saltzman/) and hear where the bones are buried. And also see the painting that was the founder's favorite. In mcgoverning with Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) , we hear about reading banned books to children at the Library of Congress. (There's tons of art in those, we hear) And former valley resident, current tv actor, fake judge, author, and comedian John Hodgman (https://www.johnhodgman.com/) talked about his new series on Hulu, "Up Here. (https://press.hulu.com/shows/up-here/) " It's a musical comedy so we can now add "singer" to his long list of accomplishments. We ask his feelings on a small historical BBC production (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I,_Claudius_(TV_series)) , just to be niche.

episodes.title: To untangle the mayor of love
episodes.text: Illumination was the name of the game for us, in that it's spring so more things should be brought to light after a full season of dark times. And to that end we begin our new series "Meet the Mayors" with Holyoke's own, Joshua Garcia (https://www.holyoke.org/contacts/joshua-a-garcia/) , who tells us not just of the fun fusion foods the St. Patrick's Day parade brings, but of many of the uphill battles the city faces as they move toward the future. It's hard to understand what's really happening with several grand juries and our former president, (even though he wasn't actually indicted or arrested today), so we asked professor at Western New England University School of Law, Jennifer Taub (https://jennifertaub.com/) , to explain the intricacies to us. And love. Where is it? What is it? We go to resident wordster, Emily Brewster (https://aceseditors.org/emily-brewster) of Merriam-Webster (https://merriam-webster.com/) to find out what the dictionary, and some enterprising patrons, have to say about the origins of the word "love". And we're taking questions and informal cases for John Hodgman (https://www.johnhodgman.com/) , who'll be on the show tomorrow. send in your question to thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or text to [1-800-639-9120](tel:18006399120) and maybe we'll ask the judge to give a verdict on your behalf!

episodes.title: A dance of kids and cheese
episodes.text: What today needs is a little action; a little something to remind us that it's spring. So we dance with the Martha Graham Company (https://marthagraham.org) as they arrive in Amherst (https://fac.umass.edu/Online/default.asp?BOparam::WScontent::loadArticle::permalink=GrahamDC&BOparam::WScontent::loadArticle::context_id=) and Storrs, CT (https://jorgensen.uconn.edu/Online/default.asp) with new and old work in tow. Artistic director Janet Eilber (https://marthagraham.org/staff/) and current company dancer Anne Souder (https://marthagraham.org/dancers/anne-souder/) show us how legacy work can still innovate, and how far Graham's technique truly reaches. Then we head off to Sunderland to Thomas Farm & Dairy, (https://www.thomasfarmstand.com) where it is kidding season. No really, Laurie Cuevas' (https://www.thomasfarmstand.com/about-us) herd has had 40+ goat babies so far this season and that's just to start. Monte and Kaliis set out to hug them all, because they are the absolute cutest, and sample their cheeses while they are at it. Plus on the show for Wednesday, we begin our 'Meet the Mayors' segment with Holyoke mayor Joshua Garcia (https://www.holyoke.org/contacts/joshua-a-garcia/) . Got a question for the mayor? Email thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or send a text to [1-800-639-9120 ](tel:18006399120)and we may just ask your burning inquiry on the show!

episodes.title: All things astronomical
episodes.text: As Sun Ra says: space is the place. So we brought folx into the studio who could bring us to the stars. Shelburne astronaut Dr. Cady Coleman (https://cadycoleman.com/) joins us to bring light to life on the space station ( she was there (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QuaIlMfB0TY) for nearly 6 months), including who gets to go these days. Also she tells us details of her training to become an astronaut, just in case you're looking into the process. The visit of one Space focused person surley deserves another. So we also have astronomer Dr. Salman Hameed (https://www.hampshire.edu/academics/faculty/salman-hameed) , Mr. Universe himself (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) , drop by to explore the details of the new spacesuits (https://www.axiomspace.com/press-kit/axemu) for the proposed moonwalk. And since our local astronaut is also a flutist making an appearance at The Drake (https://www.thedrakeamherst.org/) in Amherst this weekend with the band, Minstrels in the Gallery (https://www.minstrels.gallery/) , we invite that band's frontman Chris Devine (https://chrisdevine.com/) , resident Ian Anderson impersonator, to play a tune and expound on the appeal of Jethro Tull.

episodes.title: Extra Irish
episodes.text: Beannachtaí na Féile Pádraig oraibh uilig amuigh ansin! Ár gcéad seó le gach Aoi beo! For St. Patrick's Day, we decide to be extra Irish by learning the language! So we invite Fulbright Scholar Emer Maguire and Gerald Costello of Elms College (http://www.elms.edu) to come and teach us a few phrases. They also give us a quick look at how the language has evolved and endured, even here in western Massachusetts! Rosemary Caine (https://wildeirishwomen.com/rosie-caine-founder) delights us with her harp and some traditional and original tunes with her band The Wild Irish Shenanigans, just before their shows in Northampton and Greenfield this weekend. And Sean Barry of Four Seasons Wine & Spirits (https://fourseasonswine.com/) opened our first whiskey thunderdome! Whose spirit will reign supreme? You'll have to listen to find out!

episodes.title: Celtic pizza and congressional theater
episodes.text: We cover a lot of ground today! Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) gives us his thoughts on what's happening with the recent approval of Alaskan drilling, reparations, and pitfalls not to fall into while digging yourself out of all the snow we just got. Louise Dunphy of Celtic Crossings (https://www.facebook.com/profile.php?id=100062927396799) shows us a quick guide to all your Celtic entertainment this St. Patrick's Day weekend. Artistic director Kristen Van Ginhoven (https://www.wamtheatre.com/engage/kristen-van-ginhoven/) speaks on the importance of activism and theater working together, as her company WAM Theater (https://www.wamtheatre.com/) gears up to present "What The Constitution Means to Me" in the spring. And we eat our way through Pizza Quest II! At the behest of listener Jennifer Vose (and several others), we try out Family Pizzeria Europa (http://www.familypizzeriaeuropa.com/) here in Springfield. (and as always if you have suggestions for places we should go for Pizza Quest, email us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) or text to [800-639-9120](tel:18006399120))

episodes.title: The work of nerds
episodes.text: It's the Ides of March! Perfect time to look at the workforce! But much like Caesar getting senate approval, it's not easy to make a change if employment's what you seek. NEPM Senior Reporter Nancy Cohen (https://www.nepm.org/people/nancy-eve-cohen) shares a story about the digital divide (https://www.nepm.org/regional-news/2023-03-14/uploading-a-resume-is-a-barrier-to-employment-for-some-job-seekers-even-during-a-labor-shortage) as it applies to employment and western Massachusetts. This, of course, then inspires Monte and Kaliis to test their own digital skills, and the resiliency of recruiting websites for themselves. You may be surprised by their results. The Word Nerd, Emily Brewster leads us into arguably the most fun portion of the Merriam-Webster (https://www.merriam-webster.com/) building: the dungeon...we mean basement. It's where they keep the fun words and sometimes the ones in time out. And, inspired by our work with words this week, Kaliis suggests a few word based games of varying mediums as Nerdwatch makes a brief, but triumphant, return.

episodes.title: The winter language
episodes.text: We were all about speaking today. First we spoke to the earth, getting the low-down on winter agriculture with Alice Coleman of Stony Hill Farm (https://stonyhillfarmwilbraham.com/) in Wilbraham and Phil Korman of CISA (https://www.buylocalfood.org/) . Then we got into the guts of linguistics and how it evolves with Amherst College professor Ilan Stavans (https://www.amherst.edu/people/facstaff/istavans) through his new book "The People's Tongue: Americans and the English Language." The book features an essay by NEPM host and Merriam-Webster (https://www.merriam-webster.com/) editor-at-large Peter Sokolowski (https://www.nepm.org/people/peter-sokolowski) , so we welcomed him into the studio as well. We're also looking for our next location for Pizza Quest, so if you've got a recommendation in either North Adams or Williamstown, send an email to thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) , or text to 1-800-639-9120, and we may just feast at your spot! Disclosure: CISA is an underwriter of NEPM.

episodes.title: Rural moons and viral snow
episodes.text: To break up your Monday doldrums, we brought out some big names. That, we're told, is how you do it. Mr. Universe, Dr. Salman Hameed (https://www.hampshire.edu/academics/faculty/salman-hameed) , teaches us a lot about "moon time", and a little about how awesome the song "Naatu Naatu" is. (You can see videos about everything Monte and Mr. Universe chatted about at Kainaat Studios (https://www.kainaatstudios.com/about) ) Lt. Gov. Kim Driscoll (https://www.mass.gov/person/kim-driscoll-lieutenant-governor) visited to explain the nuances of a new position developed for the current administration: Director of Rural Affairs, and we got to nerd out about policy. Dr. Mandy Muller (https://www.micro.umass.edu/faculty-and-research/mandy-muller) gave us some clarity on where western Massachusetts is with COVID, as we celebrated the 3rd anniversary of "The Last Normal Day". And Dave Hayes, The Weather Nut (https://westernmassweather.com/) , snow-casted with us, to preview the storm tonight as it passes our way!

episodes.title: Quincy at the thunderdome crossroads
episodes.text: Today is full of exciting things to put in your weekend schedule! Local musician Kimaya Diggs (https://kimayadiggs.com) sings us selections from her brand new album "Quincy" just ahead of the CD release show at The Drake (https://www.thedrakeamherst.org/) in Amherst on Saturday. Then we enter the Wine Thunderdome once again, this time accompanied by Benson Hyde and Bruce McAmis of Provisions (https://provisionswine.com/) . We get a look at terroir as well as a preview of the selections from the NEPM Wine and Food Lovers' Weekend (https://www.nepm.org/nepm-events/2023-01-23/nepm-wine-food-lovers-weekend) , which starts tonight in Springfield. And cultural steward of the Nipmuc peoples Andre Strongbearheart Gaines Jr. (https://andrestrongbearheart.com/) teaches us about his involvement with the Smithsonian Crossroads: Museum on Main St series (https://museumonmainstreet.org/content/crossroads) , which concludes on Sunday with an event at The Shea Theater (https://sheatheater.org/) in Great Falls.

episodes.title: Shaping policy in the woods
episodes.text: There's music in the air today in the 413. First Rep. Jim McGovern (https://mcgovern.house.gov/) joins us for some mcgoverning as we ask questions from you about this state we're in and the policies being made for us. Then conductor Keith Lockhart (https://www.bso.org/keith-lockhart) makes his debut appearance as Tanglewood (https://www.bso.org/seasons/2023-tanglewood-season) correspondent! We ask him all about the new season of music up in Lenox, and about cool things happening in classical. And then real live singers in-studio as ethnomusicologist Tim Eriksen (https://www.timeriksenmusic.com/) gives us a brief lesson on shape note singing, just in time for the Western Massachusetts Sacred Harp Convention (https://wmshc.org/convention) , happening this March 11 and 12 in Northampton.

episodes.title: The prodigal dictionary
episodes.text: It's International Women's Day! Smack dab in the middle of Women's History Month! So we head to Puerto Rico to see what former Holyoke City Councilor Jossie Valentín, and her wife, Myriam Quiñones, are building there (https://www.aventurasyuiza.com) and why it felt right to leave the mainland and return home. Then we stroll around the corner to get our lexicon on with The Word Nerd at the Merriam-Webster (https://www.merriam-webster.com/) headquarters. Plus we want your questions for Rep. Jim McGovern and our upcoming Tanglewood correspondent, Keith Lockhart! Email us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) , or text to 800-639-6120 with your burning inquiries!

episodes.title: Maple forward wine and food
episodes.text: There's a Maple Sugar Moon tonight and sweetness in the air. Just in time for Massachusetts Maple Month, for which we travel to Granville to see the official first tap at Maple Corner Farm (https://maplecornerfarm.com/) . Amy Traverso (https://www.amytraverso.com/) , food editor of Yankee Magazine, joins us to discuss highlights of her career and some cool things to expect this weekend at NEPM's Wine & Food Lovers’ Weekend. And we travel north and head underground to Ten Forward (https://www.10forwardartsvenue.org/) , a new decidedly inclusive artspace helmed by Ang Buxton (and their partner Alex Noonan). They tell us all the cool things going down in their part of Greenfield, even though we were sadly remiss with the Star Trek puns.

episodes.title: Women's basketball and universal pizza
episodes.text: Today is all about competitions...sorta. We speak with head coach Lynn Hersey, director of sports and recreation Kristin Hughes, and professor Sam Intrator about the interconnected history of Smith College and women's basketball just as their current team (https://smithpioneers.com/sports/womens-basketball) win the regional championship and earn a place in the NCAA Division III’s sweet 16. Then Mr. Universe joins us to explain whether or not space is for everyone, and defend his Oscar picks. And we embark on Pizza Quest: our ongoing journey to find the best pizza western Massachusetts has to offer. Our first stop is near and dear to downtown Springfield, Red Rose (https://www.redrosepizzeria.com/) !

episodes.title: Wine tumbles and Peter Mulvey
episodes.text: As the snow times beckon once again, we give you a few things to uplift your weekend! The folx behind (https://www.sciencepodcastforkids.com/) Tumble: Science Podcast for Kids (https://www.sciencepodcastforkids.com/) give us a quick look at their new winter special coming up, and we talk about... that thing that everybody does. Then the Wine Son and Yankee Sippah invite us into their basement for the first ever "wine thunderdome": two wines enter, one wine leaves. And just ahead of his performance at the Academy of Music as a part of the John Prine tribute for the Back Porch Festival (https://www.backporchfest.com/) , Peter Mulvey (https://www.petermulvey.com) joins us in studio to talk about why Prine was so important, and to regale us with a song or two!

episodes.title: The Gr8 glad McGovern machine
episodes.text: A little old a little new, a lotta ruckus on today's show! Playwright/musician/creator extraordinaire Diana Oh joined us to discuss “My H8 for the Gr8 American Theater,” their show they've brought back to their alma mater, Smith College, this weekend. Along with Daphne Hwang Spencer, a current student and actor in the production, we take a look at some of the issues that show brings to light. The Glad Machine brings us the power-pop sounds of their latest album “Hey!” just ahead of their CD release show at The Divine Theater in Holyoke this weekend. Good rock and roll for what ails us! And we finally check in with Rep. Jim McGovern to let us know how things are going down within the beltway. ( A weekly chat with McGovern will be a regular thing here on The Fabulous 413, so if you have questions for them you’d like for us to ask, you can email us at thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) , or call/text us at 800-639-9120. )

episodes.title: Sunny words and abolition
episodes.text: It's the first day of Women's History Month! (and our first week anniversary, woo!) We talk with up-and-coming folk-punk artist Sunny War about all the cool things in their latest album “Anarchist Gospel,” and the realities of large tour schedules. Then professor Lydia Moland delved into the interesting history of another Lydia, abolitionist Lydia Maria Child, including her connection to Florence. We talk to her about her recent book on the subject. And Springfield's own wordster, Emily Brewster, the word nerd, takes us on a tour of the Merriam-Webster headquarters and shows us where the truly huge dictionaries live. Plus we read some of your notes about your snow days, which were delightful!

episodes.title: Polar tortillas on your back porch
episodes.text: This weekend Northampton will be full of great music as part of the Back Porch Festival (https://www.backporchfest.com/) . We chat with organizer Jim Olsen of Signature Sounds (http://www.signaturesounds.com/) about who's coming, how the festival is growing, and what's great about bringing music to western Massachusetts. Then it's off to a small corner of Springfield where Jorge Sosa and the crew of Mi Tierra (https://mitierratortillas.com/) make some of the best tortillas in the country out of corn grown right here in the four counties (and we try our best not to eat them all). And for all you cold weather enthusiasts, Monte joins a few locals for an icy delight: The Polar Plunge. Hear why some of your neighbors put themselves through this and hear Monte experience his first dip below the ice. Plus we wanna hear how your snow day is going! Send us a text at (800) 639-9120! Hey look! We can get texts now! IT’S THE FUTURE.

episodes.title: Former US Rep. John Olver, plus UFOs in the snow
episodes.text: What do Massachusetts State Rep. Natalie Blais (https://malegislature.gov/Legislators/Profile/NMB1) , former Northampton mayor David Narkewicz, and author Patricia Lee Lewis (http://writingretreats.org/about/) have in common? Each was an aide in to the late U.S. Rep. John Olver, who represented western Massachusetts first on Beacon Hill, and later in Washington, D.C. We speak with them about the man, his legacy, and how his passion to help his community encouraged them to do the same. We're also joined by Mr. Universe, Dr. Salman Hameed (https://www.hampshire.edu/academics/faculty/salman-hameed) , who gives us a bit of insight on UFOs as they return to the zeitgeist, including an alleged sighting right here in the Berkshires. Plus Dave Hayes, The Weather Nut, (https://westernmassweather.com/) weighs in on the storm coming through this evening and explains our sledding prospects.

episodes.title: Drinking with nuns for freedom
episodes.text: Florence, Massachusetts, has a long history of abolitionism and utopian communities. Who knew? We took at tour of the David Ruggles Center (https://davidrugglescenter.org/) to learn more of the folx who were inspired to make us a more complete union. Then we sat with local author Margot Douaihy (http://www.margotdouaihy.com/) about her recent mystery novel “Scorched Grace,” just before she stops in South Hadley for her book tour. And Ray Berry of White Lion Brewing (https://www.whitelionbrewing.com/) in Springfield makes a jaunt across the street to raise a glass with us, and discuss equity in the brewing community. Plus, we give you a run down of the recent news of Pearl St. Nightclub's liquor license and possible further changes to that town’s liquor with NEPM reporter Jill Kaufman.

episodes.title: Senators, clowns, and kings, oh my!
episodes.text: Senator Elizabeth Warren joins us to talk about environmental issues in western Massachusetts, as well as her new position as chair of the Senate Armed Services Subcommittee. Then we chat with screenwriter Sam Bromell about working with Baz Luhrmann and making music come alive on-screen for his adaptation of Elvis Presley's life and career. And how often do you get to sit with someone who has made costumes for “Wicked,” trained with “Sesame Street,” and now uses clowning to help you deal with your feelings? Today is your day as Marta Mozelle MacRostie of Brave Bucket Company meets us in studio to tell us about her latest piece: “Please Ship This Wet Gift.” We want to know how you feel about the possible changes to Northampton’s liquor and pot licenses! Send your thoughts to thefab413@nepm.org (mailto:thefab413@nepm.org) !

episodes.title: Gov. Maura Healey, GZA, and our first show
episodes.text: Our very first episode , where in addition to over-excited introductions of ourselves, we chat with Massachusetts Governor Maura Healey (https://maurahealey.com/) about where western Mass. fits in her overall plans, and GZA (https://twitter.com/TheRealGZA) about why chess is important to his engagement with his fans. We also share some of your thoughts on the ups and downs of life here in the four counties of western Massachusetts. **Program note: we missed one of our local heroes in the credits, so thank you also to Home Body for the use of their music in our show.

episodes.title: Thar be dragons
episodes.text: We talk to Bostonians about the dangers and delights of western Massachusetts. Does the border actually exist at Worcester? Are there dragons in them thar hills? Listen to The Fabulous 413 to find out!

episodes.title: Coming soon: The Fabulous 413
episodes.text: Monte Belmonte and Kaliis Smith bring you The Fabulous 413 , a new live, daily radio show and podcast celebrating life in western Massachusetts — and a kind of "Mister Rogers' Neighborhood" for grown-ups. Monte and Kaliis will introduce you to the neighbors who make our western Massachusetts the incredible place it is, with a focus on arts and agriculture, cuisine and colleges, history, happenings and whatever the people of The 413 are talking about today. We’ll explore our fascinating places from The Clark to Clarkdale Fruit Farm, Tanglewood to “The Tofu Curtain,” from Forest Park to the Five Colleges.
